addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- code-simplifier:code-simplifier: Simplifies and refines code for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. Focuses on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise. (Tools: All tools)

- docs-consistency-guard: Documentation agent that audits docs for staleness and inconsistency AND generates/updates documentation on request. Use proactively when reviewing any PR that touches CLAUDE.md, docs/adr/, CONTRIBUTING.md, or docs/architecture.md. Also invoke when the user asks "is the documentation up to date?", "update the milestone", "scaffold the ADR for X", "add this to the ADR index", "add a TODO item", "scaffold a README for X", or "create a doc stub for Y." (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write, Edit)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- migration-safety: Specialized reviewer for Alembic migrations under backend/alembic/versions/. Audits for backward-incompatible operations that would break running code during a deploy, per working-agreement (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- orchestration-abstraction-guard: Specialized reviewer that audits backend code for provider-specific branching that bypasses the OrchestrationProvider abstraction. Use this proactively when reviewing any PR that touches backend/app/orchestration/, backend/app/services/, or backend/app/api/ — especially any code path handling ADF or Airflow events, trigger bindings, or pipeline_runs. Also invoke when the user asks "does this go through the abstraction?" or similar. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-reviewer: Use this agent when you need to review code for adherence to project guidelines, style guides, and best practices. This agent should be used proactively after writing or modifying code, especially before committing changes or creating pull requests. It will check for style violations, potential issues, and ensure code follows the established patterns in CLAUDE.md. Also the agent needs to know which files to focus on for the review. In most cases this will be recently completed work which is unstaged in git (can be retrieved by running git diff). However there can be cases where this is different, make sure to specify this as the agent input when calling the agent. Typical triggers include the user asking for a review of a feature they just implemented, the assistant proactively reviewing its own newly-written code before declaring a task done, and a final pre-PR check before opening a pull request. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:code-simplifier: Use this agent when code has been written or modified and needs to be simplified for clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving all functionality. This agent should be triggered automatically after completing a coding task or writing a logical chunk of code. It simplifies code by following project best practices while retaining all functionality. The agent focuses only on recently modified code unless instructed otherwise.

Examples:

<example>
Context: The assistant has just implemented a new feature that adds user authentication to an API endpoint.
user: "Please add authentication to the /api/users endpoint"
assistant: "I've implemented the authentication for the /api/users endpoint. Here's the code:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
Since a logical chunk of code has been written (authentication feature), use the Task tool to launch the code-simplifier agent to improve the code's clarity and maintainability while preserving functionality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now let me use the code-simplifier agent to refine this implementation for better clarity and maintainability"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just fixed a bug by adding several conditional checks.
user: "Fix the null pointer exception in the data processor"
assistant: "I've added the necessary null checks to prevent the exception:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After modifying code to fix a bug, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the fix follows best practices and maintains code quality.
</commentary>
assistant: "Let me refine this bug fix using the code-simplifier agent to ensure it follows our best practices"
</example>

<example>
Context: The assistant has just refactored a function to improve performance.
user: "Optimize the data sorting algorithm for better performance"
assistant: "I've optimized the sorting algorithm. Here's the updated implementation:"
<function call omitted for brevity>
<commentary>
After completing a performance optimization task, use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and maintainable.
</commentary>
assistant: "Now I'll use the code-simplifier agent to ensure the optimized code is also clear and follows our coding standards"
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:comment-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to analyze code comments for accuracy, completeness, and long-term maintainability. This includes (1) after generating large documentation comments or docstrings, (2) before finalizing a pull request that adds or modifies comments, (3) when reviewing existing comments for potential technical debt or comment rot, and (4) when you need to verify that comments accurately reflect the code they describe. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:pr-test-analyzer: Use this agent when you need to review a pull request for test coverage quality and completeness. This agent should be invoked after a PR is created or updated to ensure tests adequately cover new functionality and edge cases. Typical triggers include the user asking whether tests on a freshly-created PR are thorough, an updated PR adding new logic that needs coverage analysis, and a final pre-merge double-check before marking a PR ready. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:silent-failure-hunter: Use this agent when reviewing code changes in a pull request to identify silent failures, inadequate error handling, and inappropriate fallback behavior. This agent should be invoked proactively after completing a logical chunk of work that involves error handling, catch blocks, fallback logic, or any code that could potentially suppress errors. Examples:

<example>
Context: Daisy has just finished implementing a new feature that fetches data from an API with fallback behavior.
Daisy: "I've added error handling to the API client. Can you review it?"
Assistant: "Let me use the silent-failure-hunter agent to thoroughly examine the error handling in your changes."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has created a PR with changes that include try-catch blocks.
Daisy: "Please review PR #1234"
Assistant: "I'll use the silent-failure-hunter agent to check for any silent failures or inadequate error handling in this PR."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example>

<example>
Context: Daisy has just refactored error handling code.
Daisy: "I've updated the error handling in the authentication module"
Assistant: "Let me proactively use the silent-failure-hunter agent to ensure the error handling changes don't introduce silent failures."
<Task tool invocation to launch silent-failure-hunter agent>
</example> (Tools: All tools)

- pr-review-toolkit:type-design-analyzer: Use this agent when you need expert analysis of type design in your codebase. Specifically use it (1) when introducing a new type to ensure it follows best practices for encapsulation and invariant expression, (2) during pull request creation to review all types being added, and (3) when refactoring existing types to improve their design quality. The agent will provide both qualitative feedback and quantitative ratings on encapsulation, invariant expression, usefulness, and enforcement. See "When to invoke" in the agent body for worked scenarios. (Tools: All tools)

- qa-verifier: QA/QE agent that (1) runs the full local verification battery mirroring CI's required checks before any commit/push/PR, (2) audits test quality on changed code — failure-mode coverage, mocked-seam smells, coverage on changed files (Week-8 ≥80% gate), and (3) exercises the running application with data-level scenarios — authoring suites/checks through the real API, negative and edge-case inputs, authz probes — against the local stack's seeded demo data. Use proactively before pushing a branch or opening a PR, after writing or modifying tests, or when the user asks "run the gate", "is this ready to push?", "is this tested enough?", or "smoke the app with bad data". (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- ui-tester: End-to-end UI QA agent that exercises DataQ's frontend on BOTH desktop and mobile viewports (functionality + rendering) against the running app, AND audits backend↔frontend feature parity — flagging backend capabilities with no frontend surface (and frontend calls with no backend). Use when the user asks to "test the UI", "check mobile rendering", "find backend/frontend gaps", "is this feature wired end-to-end", or before a release. Drives a live browser via the Playwright MCP and reads code for the parity audit. (Tools: All tools)

## claude.ai Microsoft Learn
# Microsoft Learn MCP Server

This server gives structured access to official Microsoft and Azure documentation via three tools:

## Tools

### microsoft_docs_search
Search official documentation and return up to 10 concise, high-quality content chunks (max 500 tokens each), including title, URL, and excerpt.

- Use first to get a quick, reliable overview
- Ideal for grounding answers in Microsoft knowledge

### microsoft_code_sample_search
Search for code snippets and examples in official Microsoft Learn documentation and return up to 20 relevant, high-quality code samples.

- Use when you need to provide sample Microsoft/Azure related code in your answers.  
- Ideal for generating code snippets or practical implementation examples.  
- Optional parameter `language` can filter results. 

### microsoft_docs_fetch
Fetch and convert full Microsoft documentation pages to markdown.

- Use after search when you need full content from a specific URL
- Required for detailed tutorials, troubleshooting, prerequisites, code samples, or when search results are incomplete or outdated

## Workflow

1. Use `microsoft_docs_search` to find relevant documents.
2. If you need code examples or practical snippets, use `microsoft_code_sample_search`.
3. If deeper or complete information is needed, use `microsoft_docs_fetch`.

**Search gives breadth. Code Sample Search gives practical examples. Fetch gives depth.**

All content comes from Microsoft Learn or official sources, returned in clean markdown format.

Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
=== #476 ===
[OPEN] profiler: identifier-casing & CSV-delimiter limitations in column introspection/profiling
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

Surfaced during review of #475 (column-dropdown). These are **pre-existing limitations of the profiler** that the new `list_columns` introspection path inherits/newly surfaces — tracking separately rather than blocking #475.

## 1. Identifier casing (Snowflake / Unity Catalog)
`build_columns_query` issues an unquoted `SELECT * … LIMIT 0`, so `result.keys()` returns the **warehouse-cased** names (Snowflake folds unquoted identifiers to UPPERCASE → `AMOUNT`, not `amount`). The dropdown then offers `AMOUNT`. This is arguably *correct* (those are the real stored names, and the profiler/aggregate path also emits unquoted names that fold the same way), but:
- a **quoted mixed-case** column (`"Amount"`) is stored as `Amount`; the dropdown surfaces `Amount`, but the unquoted profiler/aggregate path folds it to `AMOUNT` and cannot find it. `validate_identifier` also rejects names needing quoting.
- The combobox (#475) lets the author free-type, so this is not a hard block — but quoted/mixed-case identifiers are unsupported end-to-end in the profiler.

**Fix direction:** decide whether v1 supports quoted/mixed-case identifiers; if so, thread a quoting strategy through `validate_identifier` + the Core builders (`_table`, aggregate, top-values, columns) consistently.

## 2. CSV delimiter assumption (flat files)
`pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=0)` (column listing) and `_read_dataframe` (profiling) both use pandas defaults → **comma delimiter**. A `;`/tab-delimited CSV yields one bogus "column" (the whole header line) with no error — a silent wrong answer that defeats the dropdown for that file. Shared by both the profiler and the new lister.

**Fix direction:** sniff the delimiter (`csv.Sniffer` on the header) or accept a delimiter hint on the flat-file connection/target config; apply it consistently in `list_file_columns` and `_read_dataframe`.

Both are low-severity (combobox free-text mitigates #1; #2 matches existing profiler behavior). Post-v1.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Profiler handles case-sensitive/quoted identifiers correctly on all SQL paths
- [ ] CSV delimiter is configurable (or inferred) end-to-end in introspection + profiling
- [ ] Regression tests for both limitations

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #520 ===
[OPEN] Freshness/volume monitors don't support flat-file (ADLS/S3) datasources — SQL-only
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

## Problem
The freshness & volume monitor kinds (ADR 0012, #426/#437) are evaluated via `run_monitors`, which is implemented **only** on the SQL runners:

- `backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py` — `SnowflakeCheckRunner.run_monitors` (scalar SQL aggregate: `SELECT MAX(<col>)` / `COUNT(*)`)
- `backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py` — `UnityCatalogCheckRunner.run_monitors`

`FlatFileCheckRunner` (`backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py`) has **no** `run_monitors`. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` builds the metric as a SQL string (`build_monitor_sql` → `SELECT MAX(column) FROM {target}`), which has no meaning for a flat file read into a DataFrame.

## Impact
- You **cannot** author a freshness (or volume) monitor against an ADLS Gen2 / S3 flat-file connection — the check editor only offers Freshness/Volume for Snowflake/UC.
- Concretely: the demo/test harness (ADR 0021) seeds a deliberate **flat-file freshness** issue — `inventory_snapshot.load_ts` on the latest ADLS Parquet pushed +26h back — and DataQ has **no way to catch it**, because inventory is a flat-file-in-place (Flow C), not a warehouse table. Freshness on the *source* landing zone is arguably the most valuable place to monitor it (it's where "the source stopped producing" first shows up), and it's exactly the gap.
- Discovered while validating live freshness alerting end-to-end (freshness on Snowflake `ORDERS_HEADER.ORDER_TS` works + alerts to Slack; the flat-file equivalent is impossible).

## Direction (not prescriptive)
Give `FlatFileCheckRunner` a `run_monitors` that computes the same metrics without SQL:
- **freshness** — either (a) `MAX(<timestamp column>)` over the resolved batch DataFrame (read the column, take the max), or (b) the **file/blob `last_modified`** of the resolved batch (a "when did this file land" freshness, no column needed). (a) mirrors the SQL semantics; (b) is cheaper and catches "no new file" even when the in-file timestamps are stale/absent. Possibly offer both.
- **volume** — `len(df)` (row count) of the resolved batch — trivial once the batch is read.

The monitor-kind seam (`check.kind`, numeric `metric_value`, ADR 0012) already accommodates this; the change is a runner-level `run_monitors` impl + letting the check editor expose Freshness/Volume for flat-file connections. `monitors.py` would need a non-SQL evaluation path (the age math in `_freshness_age_hours` / `_as_aware_datetime` is already datasource-agnostic and reusable).

## Acceptance
- A freshness monitor can be authored + run against an ADLS/S3 flat-file connection and correctly flags a stale latest batch.
- A volume monitor likewise flags a thinned/short batch.
- The harness's seeded flat-file freshness + volume-drop issues on `inventory_snapshot` are caught.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `freshness` + `volume` monitors run on flat-file connections (S3 + local; ADLS code path covered by tests)
- [ ] Semantics documented (e.g. newest-file mtime for freshness; row/file counts for volume over the matched batch)
- [ ] UI no longer gates monitor kinds to SQL datasources; tests cover the new paths

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #124 ===
[OPEN] Add DQ-dimension classification to checks (Completeness/Uniqueness/Validity/…)
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement,priority/P2

## Context
Checks currently have **two** classification axes, neither of which is a data-quality *dimension*:

| Field | Axis | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `kind` | *how* the monitor works (ADR 0012) | `expectation` / `freshness` / `comparison` |
| `expectation_type` | the specific GX rule | `expect_column_values_to_be_unique` |

A **DQ dimension** (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness) is a third axis — the *semantic category* of what quality aspect a check measures. It powers dimension-rollup dashboards ("98% complete, 99.5% unique, 95% valid…"), which are a standard, valued DQ-platform feature and would feed the Week-6 health views.

We don't have it today (confirmed: no `dimension` field in the model, roadmap, or ADRs).

## Why deferred (not front-loaded)
Unlike the severity-tier `status` and threshold columns (ADR 0005) or `check.kind`/`metric_value` (ADR 0012) — which had to ride the one-shot Week-3 migration because retrofitting them is an expensive *narrowing*/data-rewrite — a `dimension` column is a **cheap additive nullable column addable anytime** (pure widening, no two-step). So there's no schema-timing pressure; it deserves a deliberate design pass rather than a rushed column in PR-B2.

## Decisions to make first (ADR-worthy)
1. **Taxonomy** — adopt the canonical 6 dimensions? (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness.) Enum/CHECK vs free-form?
2. **Stored vs auto-derived** — many GX expectations map deterministically to a dimension (`expect_column_values_to_be_unique` → Uniqueness; `…_to_not_be_null` → Completeness; `…_to_be_between`/`…_to_match_regex` → Validity). Do we **store** `dimension` on the check (user-set, override-able) or **derive** it from `expectation_type` via a lookup? Or store with a derived default?
3. **Interaction with `kind`** — freshness/`comparison` monitors map to Timeliness/Consistency respectively; make sure the mapping spans all kinds, not just GX expectations.
4. **Surface** — check editor dropdown (Week 4), dashboard dimension filter/rollup (Week 6).

## Implementation sketch (once decided)
- Additive migration: nullable `checks.dimension` (+ CHECK if enum). No data rewrite, no two-step.
- Add to check CRUD request/response schemas + a `expectation_type → dimension` lookup if auto-deriving.
- Dashboard: `GROUP BY dimension` rollup alongside the ADR-0005 health score.

## When
Post-B2 / alongside the Week-6 dashboard, or whenever dimension reporting becomes a concrete requirement. Decision recorded via PR #123 follow-up (kept out of PR-B2 deliberately).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Every check carries a DQ-dimension classification (auto-derived from expectation/monitor type, user-overridable)
- [ ] Dimension visible in the check editor and results surfaces; export/import round-trips it
- [ ] Backward-compatible migration (existing checks get a derived default)

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #889 ===
[OPEN] feat: asset DQ scorecard — per-dimension coverage + weighted score on the asset page
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement,priority/P2

## Summary
Add a DQ scorecard to the asset detail page (and a score/coverage column on the assets list): per-DQ-dimension view of **which check types are configured** (coverage) and **how many are passing** (score), rolled up over the asset's composing suites' latest runs.

**Depends on #124** (DQ-dimension classification on checks — same milestone). The scorecard is #124's consumer: dimensions land on `Check`, the scorecard aggregates by them.

## Why (feasibility assessed 2026-07-17)
- The check-level view serves the check author; a per-asset score is what a data *consumer* looks at before trusting a table, and what a lead uses to aim DQ effort. It makes the asset page (G-d: #757/#760/#773) the destination view.
- **Coverage gaps are the killer half, not the score**: "this asset has zero Timeliness or Uniqueness checks" is immediately actionable — and most real incidents are freshness/volume (CLAUDE.md §5), i.e. exactly the dimensions most likely to be uncovered.
- ~60% of the substrate exists: `AssetSummaryRead` already rolls up `checks_total`/`checks_passed`/`worst_severity` (asset_view_service._roll_up); `Run.asset_id` is stamped at dispatch so per-asset SQL aggregation over `results` needs no schema change; the ADR-0005 severity-weighted health formula exists in dashboard_service.

## Scope decisions (from the feasibility pass)
- **v1 = coverage view + equal-weight score.** Custom per-dimension weights (workspace default + per-asset override) only if demanded; per-check weights explicitly out of scope — most-configurable-surface, least value.
- **Score semantics:** severity-weighted pass rate per dimension (reuse the ADR-0005 penalty map), computed over composing suites' **latest runs** (same semantics as `_roll_up`); `skip`/`error` excluded from denominators (matches `dashboard_service._SEVERITY_STATUSES`).
- **Authz:** workspace-true score (same number for every viewer who can see the asset), with contributions from suites outside the viewer's grants shown as **redacted counts, not omitted** — the #845/#846 anonymous-neighbour precedent; a per-viewer score would silently disagree between users.
- **Prerequisite refactor:** `asset_view_service._roll_up` and `dashboard_service` are two parallel rollup implementations — unify the shared status/severity math before adding a third consumer.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] #124 landed: `Check.dimension` (derived default + stored override) available to aggregation
- [ ] Rollup unification: one shared severity/status aggregation helper used by dashboard_service, asset_view_service, and the scorecard (no third parallel formula)
- [ ] `GET /assets/{id}` extended with per-dimension scorecard: `{dimension, checks_total, checks_passing, score}` + uncovered-dimensions list
- [ ] Redaction: non-grantee contributions appear as counts only; test pins that browse/detail/scorecard agree (one-rule pattern from #847)
- [ ] Asset page scorecard panel (coverage gaps visually distinct from failing dimensions) + assets-list score column with sort
- [ ] Empty states honest: no checks ≠ score 100 (render "no coverage", not a green tick); stale runs surfaced via `last_run_at`
- [ ] Tests: SQL-level aggregation over seeded multi-suite/multi-dimension fixtures incl. skip/error exclusion + operational-failure axis (#803 split respected)
- [ ] Docs: user-docs asset page section + feature-matrix row

=== #476 ===
[OPEN] profiler: identifier-casing & CSV-delimiter limitations in column introspection/profiling
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

Surfaced during review of #475 (column-dropdown). These are **pre-existing limitations of the profiler** that the new `list_columns` introspection path inherits/newly surfaces — tracking separately rather than blocking #475.

## 1. Identifier casing (Snowflake / Unity Catalog)
`build_columns_query` issues an unquoted `SELECT * … LIMIT 0`, so `result.keys()` returns the **warehouse-cased** names (Snowflake folds unquoted identifiers to UPPERCASE → `AMOUNT`, not `amount`). The dropdown then offers `AMOUNT`. This is arguably *correct* (those are the real stored names, and the profiler/aggregate path also emits unquoted names that fold the same way), but:
- a **quoted mixed-case** column (`"Amount"`) is stored as `Amount`; the dropdown surfaces `Amount`, but the unquoted profiler/aggregate path folds it to `AMOUNT` and cannot find it. `validate_identifier` also rejects names needing quoting.
- The combobox (#475) lets the author free-type, so this is not a hard block — but quoted/mixed-case identifiers are unsupported end-to-end in the profiler.

**Fix direction:** decide whether v1 supports quoted/mixed-case identifiers; if so, thread a quoting strategy through `validate_identifier` + the Core builders (`_table`, aggregate, top-values, columns) consistently.

## 2. CSV delimiter assumption (flat files)
`pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=0)` (column listing) and `_read_dataframe` (profiling) both use pandas defaults → **comma delimiter**. A `;`/tab-delimited CSV yields one bogus "column" (the whole header line) with no error — a silent wrong answer that defeats the dropdown for that file. Shared by both the profiler and the new lister.

**Fix direction:** sniff the delimiter (`csv.Sniffer` on the header) or accept a delimiter hint on the flat-file connection/target config; apply it consistently in `list_file_columns` and `_read_dataframe`.

Both are low-severity (combobox free-text mitigates #1; #2 matches existing profiler behavior). Post-v1.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Profiler handles case-sensitive/quoted identifiers correctly on all SQL paths
- [ ] CSV delimiter is configurable (or inferred) end-to-end in introspection + profiling
- [ ] Regression tests for both limitations

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #520 ===
[OPEN] Freshness/volume monitors don't support flat-file (ADLS/S3) datasources — SQL-only
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement

## Problem
The freshness & volume monitor kinds (ADR 0012, #426/#437) are evaluated via `run_monitors`, which is implemented **only** on the SQL runners:

- `backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py` — `SnowflakeCheckRunner.run_monitors` (scalar SQL aggregate: `SELECT MAX(<col>)` / `COUNT(*)`)
- `backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py` — `UnityCatalogCheckRunner.run_monitors`

`FlatFileCheckRunner` (`backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py`) has **no** `run_monitors`. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` builds the metric as a SQL string (`build_monitor_sql` → `SELECT MAX(column) FROM {target}`), which has no meaning for a flat file read into a DataFrame.

## Impact
- You **cannot** author a freshness (or volume) monitor against an ADLS Gen2 / S3 flat-file connection — the check editor only offers Freshness/Volume for Snowflake/UC.
- Concretely: the demo/test harness (ADR 0021) seeds a deliberate **flat-file freshness** issue — `inventory_snapshot.load_ts` on the latest ADLS Parquet pushed +26h back — and DataQ has **no way to catch it**, because inventory is a flat-file-in-place (Flow C), not a warehouse table. Freshness on the *source* landing zone is arguably the most valuable place to monitor it (it's where "the source stopped producing" first shows up), and it's exactly the gap.
- Discovered while validating live freshness alerting end-to-end (freshness on Snowflake `ORDERS_HEADER.ORDER_TS` works + alerts to Slack; the flat-file equivalent is impossible).

## Direction (not prescriptive)
Give `FlatFileCheckRunner` a `run_monitors` that computes the same metrics without SQL:
- **freshness** — either (a) `MAX(<timestamp column>)` over the resolved batch DataFrame (read the column, take the max), or (b) the **file/blob `last_modified`** of the resolved batch (a "when did this file land" freshness, no column needed). (a) mirrors the SQL semantics; (b) is cheaper and catches "no new file" even when the in-file timestamps are stale/absent. Possibly offer both.
- **volume** — `len(df)` (row count) of the resolved batch — trivial once the batch is read.

The monitor-kind seam (`check.kind`, numeric `metric_value`, ADR 0012) already accommodates this; the change is a runner-level `run_monitors` impl + letting the check editor expose Freshness/Volume for flat-file connections. `monitors.py` would need a non-SQL evaluation path (the age math in `_freshness_age_hours` / `_as_aware_datetime` is already datasource-agnostic and reusable).

## Acceptance
- A freshness monitor can be authored + run against an ADLS/S3 flat-file connection and correctly flags a stale latest batch.
- A volume monitor likewise flags a thinned/short batch.
- The harness's seeded flat-file freshness + volume-drop issues on `inventory_snapshot` are caught.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `freshness` + `volume` monitors run on flat-file connections (S3 + local; ADLS code path covered by tests)
- [ ] Semantics documented (e.g. newest-file mtime for freshness; row/file counts for volume over the matched batch)
- [ ] UI no longer gates monitor kinds to SQL datasources; tests cover the new paths

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

=== #124 ===
[OPEN] Add DQ-dimension classification to checks (Completeness/Uniqueness/Validity/…)
milestone: v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind | labels: enhancement,priority/P2

## Context
Checks currently have **two** classification axes, neither of which is a data-quality *dimension*:

| Field | Axis | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `kind` | *how* the monitor works (ADR 0012) | `expectation` / `freshness` / `comparison` |
| `expectation_type` | the specific GX rule | `expect_column_values_to_be_unique` |

A **DQ dimension** (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness) is a third axis — the *semantic category* of what quality aspect a check measures. It powers dimension-rollup dashboards ("98% complete, 99.5% unique, 95% valid…"), which are a standard, valued DQ-platform feature and would feed the Week-6 health views.

We don't have it today (confirmed: no `dimension` field in the model, roadmap, or ADRs).

## Why deferred (not front-loaded)
Unlike the severity-tier `status` and threshold columns (ADR 0005) or `check.kind`/`metric_value` (ADR 0012) — which had to ride the one-shot Week-3 migration because retrofitting them is an expensive *narrowing*/data-rewrite — a `dimension` column is a **cheap additive nullable column addable anytime** (pure widening, no two-step). So there's no schema-timing pressure; it deserves a deliberate design pass rather than a rushed column in PR-B2.

## Decisions to make first (ADR-worthy)
1. **Taxonomy** — adopt the canonical 6 dimensions? (Completeness, Uniqueness, Validity, Accuracy, Consistency, Timeliness.) Enum/CHECK vs free-form?
2. **Stored vs auto-derived** — many GX expectations map deterministically to a dimension (`expect_column_values_to_be_unique` → Uniqueness; `…_to_not_be_null` → Completeness; `…_to_be_between`/`…_to_match_regex` → Validity). Do we **store** `dimension` on the check (user-set, override-able) or **derive** it from `expectation_type` via a lookup? Or store with a derived default?
3. **Interaction with `kind`** — freshness/`comparison` monitors map to Timeliness/Consistency respectively; make sure the mapping spans all kinds, not just GX expectations.
4. **Surface** — check editor dropdown (Week 4), dashboard dimension filter/rollup (Week 6).

## Implementation sketch (once decided)
- Additive migration: nullable `checks.dimension` (+ CHECK if enum). No data rewrite, no two-step.
- Add to check CRUD request/response schemas + a `expectation_type → dimension` lookup if auto-deriving.
- Dashboard: `GROUP BY dimension` rollup alongside the ADR-0005 health score.

## When
Post-B2 / alongside the Week-6 dashboard, or whenever dimension reporting becomes a concrete requirement. Decision recorded via PR #123 follow-up (kept out of PR-B2 deliberately).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Every check carries a DQ-dimension classification (auto-derived from expectation/monitor type, user-overridable)
- [ ] Dimension visible in the check editor and results surfaces; export/import round-trips it
- [ ] Backward-compatible migration (existing checks get a derived default)

_(AC added at v1.1 planning, 2026-07-04.)_

In /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ, do a thorough recon for two issues:

#520 — freshness/volume monitors are SQL-only, need a flat-file impl.
#476 — profiler identifier-casing (Snowflake/UC quoted mixed-case) + CSV delimiter assumption.

Report precisely (file:line):
1. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` — full structure: what `build_monitor_sql`, `_freshness_age_hours`, `_as_aware_datetime` do, and the exact interface/signature `run_monitors` has on SnowflakeCheckRunner and UnityCatalogCheckRunner. What return type do they produce?

#889 — asset DQ scorecard: per-dimension coverage + weighted score on the asset detail page + assets list column.

Report precisely (file:line):

content.text:
Recon complete. Findings below (all paths absolute).

## 1. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` (438 lines)

**Structure:** pure, DB-free core + a per-kind strategy registry (#726).

- **`build_monitor_sql(kind, *, table, schema, catalog, config) -> str`** — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/monitors.py:95-110`. Looks up `_strategy(kind)`; if `strategy.build_sql is None` raises `MonitorConfigError(f"monitor kind {kind!r} has no scalar-SQL form")` (:108). Builds the target via `qualified_table` (:74-92, which rejects catalog-without-schema at :82-86), then delegates to the strategy's `build_sql`. Concrete builders: `_freshness_sql` at :202-204 → `f"SELECT MAX({column}) FROM {target}"`; `_volume_sql` at :239-240 → `f"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {target}"`. Identifier safety comes only from `_ident` (:62-71) → `is_sql_identifier`.
- **`_freshness_age_hours(max_timestamp, now) -> float`** — :113-116. `max((now - max_timestamp).total_seconds() / 3600.0, 0.0)` — clamps clock-skew futures to 0.
- **`_as_aware_datetime(scalar, column) -> datetime`** — :119-134. Accepts `datetime`, else `date` → `datetime.combine(scalar, time.min)` (:129, note `datetime` checked first since it subclasses `date`), else raises `MonitorConfigError`. Naive → `.replace(tzinfo=UTC)` (:134).
- **Registry:** `MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY` :317-327 with `MonitorKindStrategy` dataclass (:302-314: `kind`, `validate_config`, `outcome`, `build_sql | None`). Derived tuples at :337-342: `MONITOR_KINDS`, `SCALAR_MONITOR_KINDS` (`build_sql is not None` → freshness, volume), `STATEFUL_MONITOR_KINDS` (→ schema_drift). Registration is import-time only (comment :331-336).
- **Shared execution helpers:** `run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, *, monitors, now) -> list[CheckOutcome]` :352-385 (datasource-agnostic banding loop, per-monitor error isolation at :376-384); `evaluate_monitors(fetch_scalar, ...)` :388-409; `run_monitors_over_engine(engine, *, table, schema, catalog, monitors)` :412-438 (opens ONE connection, `conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar()`).

**`run_monitors` interface** — declared on the `MonitorRunner` Protocol at `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/base.py:128-134`:

```python
supported_monitor_kinds: frozenset[str]

def run_monitors(
    self, *, table: str, schema: str | None, monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
) -> list[CheckOutcome]: ...
```

Return type is **`list[CheckOutcome]`**, one per `MonitorSpec`, in order (not a `SuiteOutcome`).

- `SnowflakeCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py:261-276`; delegates to `run_monitors_over_engine(self._engine.get(), table=table, schema=schema or self._config.schema_, catalog=None, monitors=monitors)`. Capability at `snowflake.py:179`: `supported_monitor_kinds: ClassVar[frozenset[str]] = frozenset({FRESHNESS, VOLUME})`.
- `UnityCatalogCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py:198-212`; same but `catalog=self._catalog` (3-part name). Capability at `unity_catalog.py:137`.
- Precedent for a **non-SQL** implementation: `IcebergCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py:336-360+`. It never builds SQL: loads the table once *before* the loop, then uses `run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, monitors=monitors, now=datetime.now(UTC))` with a native `_monitor_scalar`. **This is the exact template for a flat-file impl (#520)** — `run_monitor_specs` is the reusable seam.

## 2. `backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py` (282 lines)

- **`FlatFileCheckRunner`** — :121-157. Holds `conn_type`/`config`/`secret`. Only implements `run_checks` (:134-157) — **no `run_monitors`, no `supported_monitor_kinds`**. `table` carries the file path; `schema` unused (:126-127).
- **File read:** module-level `read_dataframe(*, conn_type, config, path, secret)` — :102-118 (note: public name, not `_read_dataframe`; the private `_read_dataframe` names are in `iceberg.py:302` and `profile_service.py:602`). Reads the **whole** file: `pd.read_csv(raw)` (:117) or `pd.read_parquet(raw, dtype_backend="pyarrow")` (:118). Format from `format_from_path` (:50-57, `.csv` / `.parquet|.pq` only).
  - **#476 delimiter assumption lives here at :117** — bare `pd.read_csv(raw)`, no `sep=`. Grep across `backend/app` confirms *every* CSV read hardcodes the pandas default comma: `flatfile.py:117`, `schema_drift.py:203`, `profile_service.py:638`, `profile_service.py:960`. No `sep=`/`delimiter=` anywhere in backend.
- **`list_file_columns` is NOT in this file** — it's `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/services/profile_service.py:934-969` (header-only: `pd.read_csv(raw, nrows=0).columns` at :960, or `pq.ParquetFile(raw).schema.names` at :963).
- **Batch resolution path** — :176-282:
  - `FileRef` dataclass :189-195 — `path: str`, `last_modified: datetime | None = None`.
  - `resolve_batch(files, *, pattern, strategy="latest", batch=None) -> str` :202-242 — pure; regex first capture group is the batch key; `latest` picks max key lexicographically (:241) or falls back to `_most_recent` (:199, `max` on `(last_modified or _MIN_DT, path)`); `specific` at :225-231.
  - `list_files(*, conn_type, config, prefix, secret) -> list[FileRef]` :245-267 — **the live seam, and this is where blob mtime IS available**:
    - **s3** (:249-256): `FileRef(path=obj["Key"], last_modified=obj.get("LastModified"))` — boto3 `list_objects_v2` paginator; mtime always present in practice.
    - **adls_gen2** (:258-267): `FileRef(path=blob.name, last_modified=getattr(blob, "last_modified", None))` — `container.list_blobs(name_starts_with=prefix)`.
    - **local**: there is **no local flat-file adapter**. `_FILE_TYPES = {"adls_gen2", "s3"}` (:44). Only `adls.py` and `s3.py` exist.
  - `resolve_batch_file(...)` :270-282 — list + resolve, called by `run_target.materialize_path` (`/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/services/run_target.py:150-186`, the `flatfile.resolve_batch_file` call at :178).
  - `download_bytes` :81-99 — object download; note **`download_bytes` does not return metadata**, so freshness-from-blob-mtime would need `list_files` (or a new head/properties call), not `download_bytes`.

## 3. Gating of monitor kinds

**Frontend** — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts:374-392`, function `expectationsByCategoryFor`. Exact condition:


## 1. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` (438 lines)

Builds the target via `qualified_table` (:74-92, which rejects catalog-without-schema at :82-86), then delegates to the strategy's `build_sql`. Concrete builders: `_freshness_sql` at :202-204 → `f"SELECT MAX({column}) FROM {target}"`; `_volume_sql` at :239-240 → `f"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {target}"`. Identifier safety comes only from `_ident` (:62-71) → `is_sql_identifier`.
- **`_freshness_age_hours(max_timestamp, now) -> float`** — :113-116. `max((now - max_timestamp).total_seconds() / 3600.0, 0.0)` — clamps clock-skew futures to 0.
- **`_as_aware_datetime(scalar, column) -> datetime`** — :119-134. Accepts `datetime`, else `date` → `datetime.combine(scalar, time.min)` (:129, note `datetime` checked first since it subclasses `date`), else raises `MonitorConfigError`. Naive → `.replace(tzinfo=UTC)` (:134).
- **Registry:** `MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY` :317-327 with `MonitorKindStrategy` dataclass (:302-314: `kind`, `validate_config`, `outcome`, `build_sql | None`). Derived tuples at :337-342: `MONITOR_KINDS`, `SCALAR_MONITOR_KINDS` (`build_sql is not None` → freshness, volume), `STATEFUL_MONITOR_KINDS` (→ schema_drift). Registration is import-time only (comment :331-336).
- **Shared execution helpers:** `run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, *, monitors, now) -> list[CheckOutcome]` :352-385 (datasource-agnostic banding loop, per-monitor error isolation at :376-384); `evaluate_monitors(fetch_scalar, ...)` :388-409; `run_monitors_over_engine(engine, *, table, schema, catalog, monitors)` :412-438 (opens ONE connection, `conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar()`).

**`run_monitors` interface** — declared on the `MonitorRunner` Protocol at `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/base.py:128-134`:

```python
supported_monitor_kinds: frozenset[str]

def run_monitors(
    self, *, table: str, schema: str | None, monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
) -> list[CheckOutcome]: ...
```

Return type is **`list[CheckOutcome]`**, one per `MonitorSpec`, in order (not a `SuiteOutcome`).

- `SnowflakeCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py:261-276`; delegates to `run_monitors_over_engine(self._engine.get(), table=table, schema=schema or self._config.schema_, catalog=None, monitors=monitors)`. Capability at `snowflake.py:179`: `supported_monitor_kinds: ClassVar[frozenset[str]] = frozenset({FRESHNESS, VOLUME})`.
- `UnityCatalogCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/unity_catalog.py:198-212`; same but `catalog=self._catalog` (3-part name). Capability at `unity_catalog.py:137`.
- Precedent for a **non-SQL** implementation: `IcebergCheckRunner.run_monitors` — `/Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ/backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py:336-360+`. It never builds SQL: loads the table once *before* the loop, then uses `run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, monitors=monitors, now=datetime.now(UTC))` with a native `_monitor_scalar`. **This is the exact template for a flat-file impl (#520)** — `run_monitor_specs` is the reusable seam.

## 2. `backend/app/datasources/flatfile.py` (282 lines)

content.text:
Recon complete. Here is the map.

## 1. `Check` model — `backend/app/db/models.py`

**Enum/CHECK-constraint precedent** (`models.py:120-122`) — the one idiom for a new enum column:
```python
def _in_check(column: str, values: tuple[str, ...], name: str) -> CheckConstraint:
    quoted = ", ".join(f"'{v}'" for v in values)
    return CheckConstraint(f"{column} IN ({quoted})", name=name)
```
Vocabulary tuples all live at module top: `RUN_STATUSES:37`, `_RESULT_SEVERITY_TIERS:44`, `RESULT_OPERATIONAL_STATUSES:45`, `RESULT_STATUSES:46`, `SEVERITY_RANK:53`, `FAILING_TIERS:59`, `worst_severity():62`, `CHECK_KINDS:78`, `COMPARISON_KIND:79`, `PERMISSIONS:82`, `ENVS:83`. A `DQ_DIMENSIONS` tuple belongs here at ~line 78, next to `CHECK_KINDS`.

`class Check` — `models.py:407-456`. `__table_args__` (409-420): `_in_check("kind", CHECK_KINDS, "kind_valid")`, the `comparison_source_presence` CheckConstraint, two indexes. Columns:
- `id:422`, `suite_id:423` (CASCADE), `name:426` (String(256))
- `kind:429-431` — `String(32), nullable=False, server_default=text("'expectation'")` — **the exact shape a new `dimension` column should copy**
- `expectation_type:432` — `String(128), nullable=False`
- `source_connection_id:438`, `warn/fail/critical_threshold:442-444` (Numeric), `config:445` (JSONB), `alert_snoozed_until:452`, `created_at/updated_at:453-454`

**There is no `engine` column yet.** It is specified but unbuilt in `docs/adr/0036-connection-anchored-check-engines.md` §2: `check.engine` (TEXT, default `'gx'`, additive migration). ADR 0036 §4 (line 45) explicitly ties #124 and #889 to this work:

> "This keeps the ADR 0012 seam, the scorecard's dimension mapping (#124/#889), and trend queries engine-agnostic… the expectation catalog (frontend-only today, by design) must become an **engine-aware backend catalog**; that is one story with #124's dimension classification, not two catalogs."

And line 70: "The scorecard (#889) and all trend/aggregation SQL stay engine-agnostic — guaranteed by §4." No `dimension` token exists anywhere in backend/frontend/docs today (greenfield).

`CheckVersion` — `models.py:501-551`, snapshot columns `name/kind/expectation_type/source_connection_id/config/thresholds` at `526-536`. A new `dimension` column must be added here too or history loses it.

`MonitorBaseline:459` also carries `_in_check("kind", CHECK_KINDS, ...)` at `:479`.

## 2. Check CRUD

**Schemas** — `backend/app/api/v1/checks.py`:
- `CheckCreate:41-53` (`kind: str = "expectation"` at :45, `expectation_type` :46)
- `CheckUpdate:56-65` — note `kind` is **absent**: kind is immutable on PATCH. A `dimension` field is a design decision here (mutable vs not).
- `CheckRead:68-83`
- `CheckVersionRead:262-282`, `CheckDryRunRequest:338-348`

**Service** — `backend/app/services/check_service.py`:
- `validate_kind:128`, `validate_lengths:150`, `validate_monitor_check:169`, `validate_comparison_check:299`, `validate_expectation_check:432`
- `record_check_version:478-509` — copies every editable field into a `CheckVersion` (`:495-507`)
- `create_check:512-582` — validate → construct `Check(...)` (`:565`) → `flush()` → `record_check_version` → commit
- `update_check:603-719` — "compute effective post-patch values and validate BEFORE touching the ORM object" (`:632-636`); `if session.is_modified(check): record_check_version(...)` (`:700-701`); `IntegrityError` → `CheckEditConflictError` 409 on `uq_check_versions_check_version`

**Router** — `checks.py:86` create, `:115` list, `:129` get, `:144` patch, `:173` delete, `:191` rebaseline, `:226/:243` snooze, `:285` versions, `:316` history, `:358` dryrun. All gated by `require_permission(db, suite_id, current_user.id, minimum="view"|"edit")`.

**Export/import round-trip** — `backend/app/services/suite_io_service.py`:
- `EXPORT_VERSION = 1` (`:48`) — "Bump when the document shape changes incompatibly"
- `export_suite:61-97` — the per-check doc dict is built at `:76-84` with exactly `name/kind/expectation_type/config/warn/fail/critical`. **Adding `dimension` requires touching this dict.**
- `import_suite:126-239` — validates all before writing; `Check(...)` construction at `:215-224`; `record_check_version` per check at `:229-230`
- Wire models: `backend/app/api/v1/suites.py` — `CheckDocument:283-294`, `SuiteDocument:297-304`, `export_suite:317`, `import_suite:332`

Also writes checks: `backend/app/mcp/server.py:350-397` (`create_check` MCP tool, `_reject_nul:95`).

## 3. `_roll_up` — `backend/app/services/asset_view_service.py:343-394`

Full body quoted in the file; the substance:
- Input: `list[RunOutcome]` for **all** composing suites (never grant-filtered).
- Accumulates `statuses` from each `run.worst_severity` (`:353-354`), then `worst_severity(statuses)` at `:385` — the shared `models.worst_severity` helper (rank-based, `critical > fail > warn`).
- `checks_total += run.checks_total; checks_passed += run.checks_passed` (`:371-372`) — **plain summation across suites**, no weighting, no per-dimension breakdown.
- `last_run_at` = max of `run.finished_at or run.created_at` (`:373-375`).
- Execution flags `:358-363`; connection-health flags `:367-370` (`failed or has_error → has_operational_error`; `has_skip`).

Upstream data path: `_latest_run_per_suite:265` (DISTINCT ON) → `_latest_outcomes:326-340` → `check_outcome_counts` + `operational_result_flags` (both in `run_service.py:385` / `:422`). `check_outcome_counts` returns `(total, passed, worst)` where total = `pass` + the three failing tiers, **excluding** `skip`/`error` (`run_service.py:412-418`).

**The one-rule authz/redaction pattern** (module docstring `:7-24`) — the ADR 0037 three-layer rule superseding #845/#846/#920:
- Identity & topology: workspace knowledge, never redacted.
- **Aggregate verdicts are workspace-true** — the rollup is computed over ALL composing suites for every viewer, explicitly citing "#889's two-verdicts-on-one-page problem" (`:16-17`). **A per-dimension scorecard must obey this: aggregate over all suites, not the caller's.**
- Itemized evaluation stays behind ADR 0027 suite grants; the rest collapse to `restricted_suite_count`.

Enforcement site: `get_visible_asset:431-483` — `effective_permissions:456` is the ONE visibility derivation (`:450-455` comment: "a suite is listed iff it has a label"); `summary = _roll_up(asset, [outcome_by_suite[s.id] for s in all_suites])` at `:464` uses `all_suites`, while `composing` uses `visible`. `list_visible_assets:400-428` takes no user at all.

## 4. `dashboard_service.py` — the ADR-0005 formula

- `_PENALTY:33-38` — `{pass:0.0, warn:0.5, fail:1.0, critical:2.0}`, `_W_MAX = 2.0` (`:39`)
- `_SEVERITY_STATUSES:43` — `tuple(_PENALTY)`, i.e. the four tiers; `skip`/`error` excluded from N
- `health_score:50-62` — `round(100.0 * (1.0 - penalty / (n * _W_MAX)), 1)`, `None` when n == 0
- `pass_rate:65-71`, `performance_state:74-82` (bands `_OPTIMAL_MIN=90.0`, `_STABLE_MIN=60.0` at `:46-47`)
- Parallel rollup math: `_status_counts:135-154` (histogram of `Result.status` joined to `Run`, windowed) and `_suite_performance:197-237` — its own DISTINCT ON latest-run subquery (`:205-212`) duplicating `asset_view_service._latest_run_per_suite:265`.

**What would be unified for #889:**
1. The **latest-run-per-suite DISTINCT ON** exists twice (`dashboard_service.py:205-212` vs `asset_view_service.py:269-274`).
2. `health_score`/`pass_rate` live only in `dashboard_service`, keyed off a `{status: count}` histogram — but `asset_view_service._roll_up` never builds a histogram, only `(total, passed, worst)`. A weighted asset score needs the full four-tier histogram, so either `check_outcome_counts` must return the histogram (it already computes `by_run[run_id][status]` internally at `run_service.py:407-409` and throws it away) or `_roll_up` must gain one.
3. `models.SEVERITY_RANK` (discrete order) vs `dashboard._PENALTY` (continuous weights) are deliberately separate concepts — `models.py:47-52` and `dashboard_service.py:28-30` both say so. `health_score` is the natural thing to lift into a shared module (it's pure, `Mapping[str,int] → float|None`); `_PENALTY` should NOT be merged into `SEVERITY_RANK`.
4. Scope difference to reconcile: dashboard is **grant-scoped** (`suite_service.accessible_suite_ids`, `:312`), asset view is **workspace-true**. A shared scorer must take the histogram as an argument and stay scope-agnostic.

## 5. `Run.asset_id` + `results` columns

- `Run.asset_id` — `models.py:589-591`, `ondelete="SET NULL"`, nullable, indexed (`ix_runs_asset_id`, `:560`). Docstring `:585-588`: "stamped at dispatch (ADR 0034)… never rewrites when a suite's target later changes."
- Stamping: `backend/app/services/run_dispatch.py:32-45` — `new_queued_run` sets `asset_id=suite.asset_id` (`:43`); the docstring warns `orchestration_service` builds its Run via `pg_insert` with "a matching inline `asset_id` subquery — keep the two in sync."
- `Result` — `models.py:607-637`: `run_id:616` (CASCADE, `ix_results_run_id`), `check_id:622` (CASCADE, `ix_results_check_id`), `status:625` (CHECK over `RESULT_STATUSES`), `metric_value:629` (Numeric), `duration_ms:630`, `observed_value/expected_value/sample_failures:633-635` (JSONB `none_as_null`), `created_at:637`.

Note for #889: a per-dimension aggregate needs `results ⋈ checks` on `check_id` (the dimension lives on the check, not the result). `ix_results_check_id` exists; `Run.asset_id` lets you go asset → runs directly without the suite hop.

## 6. Frontend

**Check editor** (where `expectation_type`/`kind` are chosen):
- `frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts` — the catalog. `CheckKind` type at `:27`; `ExpectationCategory:37` and `EXPECTATION_CATEGORIES:39-48` (`'Column values' | 'Table shape' | 'Freshness' | 'Volume' | 'Schema' | 'Custom SQL' | 'Comparison'`) — **an existing classification axis, orthogonal to DQ dimensions**; `ExpectationSpec:80-92`; `EXPECTATION_BY_TYPE` lookup at `:337`. This is the file ADR 0036 §4 says must become a backend catalog.
- `frontend/src/pages/CheckNew.tsx` — 3-step wizard: category → expectation → config; `spec` at `:54`; submit `:63-76`
- `frontend/src/pages/CheckEdit.tsx` — `expectation_type` Select at `:173-190`, prefill `:125-126`
- `frontend/src/components/checks/checkForm.ts` — `buildCheckPayload:63-76` (`kind: spec?.kind ?? 'expectation'`), `buildComparisonPayload:81-114`
- `frontend/src/components/checks/CheckHistoryDrawer.tsx:33` — version drawer
- Wire types: `frontend/src/api/suites.ts:121,150,162,216,271,308`

**Asset detail** — `frontend/src/pages/AssetDetail.tsx` (509 lines): `AssetDetailBody:93`; the two-axis header `HealthAxis` calls at `:128-137`; `HealthAxis` component `:212-225`; `DescriptionBlock:231`, `OwnerBlock:317`, `SuitesSection:414` (per-suite `checks_passed / checks_total` render at `:459`), then `IncidentsPanel`, `LineageGraph`, `ColumnLineagePanel`. The scorecard slots naturally between the header and `SuitesSection`.

**Assets list** — `frontend/src/pages/Assets.tsx`: `AssetsTable:166`, `columns:167-213` — Asset / Env / Suites (`suite_count`, `:191-196`) / Health (`AssetHealthTag`, `:197-202`) / Last seen. A new score column goes here.

**Health derivation** — `frontend/src/components/assets/health.ts`: `assetHealth:41-52`, `connectionHealth:74-94`, `suiteHealth:110-124`, `runHealth:127-134`. `suiteHealth` is the label-only analogue of what #889 wants to make numeric. Also consumed by `frontend/src/components/dashboard/AssetHealthLead.tsx:198`.

API client types: `frontend/src/api/assets.ts` (`AssetSummary`, `RunOutcome`).

## 7. Alembic

**Head revision: `a3b4c5d6e7f8`** — `backend/alembic/versions/a3b4c5d6e7f8_pipeline_runs_connection_fk_cascade.py` (34 revisions total, single head, verified by set difference of revision/down_revision).

Naming convention: `<12-hex-revision-id>_<snake_case_slug>.py`. Ids are hand-authored hex-looking strings (e.g. `d1e2f3a4b5c6`, `c4d5e6f7a8b9`), not `alembic revision` autogenerated hashes — pick one in that style. File header convention (`d1e2f3a4b5c6_connection_poll_health.py:1-40`): a prose docstring with `Revision ID:` / `Revises:` / `Create Date:` plus a rationale section, then `revision = "..."`, `down_revision = "..."`, `branch_labels = None`, `depends_on = None`. Newer files use bare `revision = ` while older use `revision: str = `; both present. Backward-compat rule stated inline at `:32-35`: "all three are nullable or carry a server default, so the currently-deployed code — which never writes them — keeps working against this schema… (two-step deploy)."

Precedent migrations to copy for a nullable-with-default enum column: `b7c8d9e0f1a2_add_check_source_connection.py`, `c4d5e6f7a8b9_add_check_versions.py`.

## 8. Existing tests

- `backend/tests/services/test_asset_view_service.py` (208 lines) — `test_summarize_asset_with_no_suites:44`, `test_error_result_feeds_connection_health_not_suite_health:136`, `test_skip_result_is_degraded_not_an_error:150`, `test_failed_run_is_an_operational_error:163`, `test_failing_data_does_not_touch_connection_health:174`, `test_cancelled_run_is_flagged_so_it_never_rolls_up_green:189`
- `backend/tests/services/test_dashboard_service.py` (336 lines) — parametrized `test_health_score:37`, `test_health_score_excludes_skip_and_error_from_n:41`, `test_pass_rate:56`, `test_performance_state:72`, plus scoping/window/delta tests `:130-336`
- `backend/tests/api/test_checks.py` (1444 lines) — the big CRUD + validation + version-history suite
- `backend/tests/api/test_assets.py` (789 lines) — asset endpoint + ADR 0037 workspace-true assertions
- `backend/tests/api/test_dashboard.py` (131), `backend/tests/api/test_suites.py` (export/import round-trip), `backend/tests/api/test_comparison_checks.py`, `backend/tests/services/test_run_service.py`, `backend/tests/services/test_severity.py`

---

**Two design constraints worth flagging up front.** First, ADR 0036 (`docs/adr/0036-connection-anchored-check-engines.md`, Accepted 2026-07-17) names #124 and #889 by number and asserts the dimension catalog and the engine-aware backend catalog are "one story… not two catalogs" — so #124 as scoped here may be a slice of a larger already-decided design rather than a standalone column. Second, `asset_view_service`'s module docstring cites "#889's two-verdicts-on-one-page problem" as the *reason* the rollup is workspace-true; a per-dimension scorecard that filtered by caller grants would reintroduce exactly the bug ADR 0037 was written to kill.
