# ───────────────── DQ dimension (ADR 0038, #124) ────────────────────


def test_create_derives_the_dimension_when_unspecified(
    client: TestClient, db_session: Any
) -> None:
    """The common path: nobody should hand-classify a not-null check."""
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    resp = client.post(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_payload())
    assert resp.status_code == 201
    assert resp.json()["dimension"] == "completeness"


def test_create_honours_an_explicit_dimension_over_the_derived_one(
    client: TestClient, db_session: Any
) -> None:
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    resp = client.post(
        f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_payload(dimension="accuracy")
    )
    assert resp.status_code == 201
    assert resp.json()["dimension"] == "accuracy"


def test_create_leaves_an_underivable_check_unclassified(
    client: TestClient, db_session: Any
) -> None:
    """NULL is a real state (ADR 0038 §3), not a failure — a custom-SQL predicate
    genuinely cannot be classified, and the scorecard must see the gap."""
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    resp = client.post(
        f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks",
        json=_payload(
            name="custom",
            expectation_type="unexpected_rows_expectation",
            config={"unexpected_rows_query": "SELECT 1 WHERE false"},
        ),
    )
    assert resp.status_code == 201
    assert resp.json()["dimension"] is None


@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad", ["Completeness", "timeliness ", "freshness", "nonsense"])
def test_create_rejects_a_non_canonical_dimension(
    client: TestClient, db_session: Any, bad: str
) -> None:
    """The vocabulary is closed so coverage reporting can be truthful — a typo'd
    'timeliness ' would make "you have no Timeliness checks" a lie. Note
    'freshness' is a KIND, not a dimension: the axes are easy to confuse."""
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    resp = client.post(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_payload(dimension=bad))
    assert resp.status_code == 422
    assert resp.json()["error"]["code"] == "check_config_invalid"


def test_dimension_is_reclassifiable_by_patch(client: TestClient, db_session: Any) -> None:
    """ADR 0038 §2 — derivation is a guess about intent, so the override must be
    changeable after creation, not only at authoring time."""
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    cid = client.post(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_payload()).json()["id"]
    resp = client.patch(
        f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks/{cid}", json={"dimension": "integrity"}
    )
    assert resp.status_code == 200
    assert resp.json()["dimension"] == "integrity"


def test_patch_rejects_a_non_canonical_dimension(client: TestClient, db_session: Any) -> None:
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    cid = client.post(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_payload()).json()["id"]
    resp = client.patch(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks/{cid}", json={"dimension": "nope"})
    assert resp.status_code == 422
    assert client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks/{cid}").json()["dimension"] == (
        "completeness"
    )  # unchanged — a rejected PATCH must leave nothing dirty


def test_patch_without_a_dimension_does_not_clear_it(
    client: TestClient, db_session: Any
) -> None:
    """PATCH convention: None = "not provided". A rename must not silently wipe
    the classification."""
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    cid = client.post(
        f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_payload(dimension="accuracy")
    ).json()["id"]
    resp = client.patch(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks/{cid}", json={"name": "renamed"})
    assert resp.status_code == 200
    assert resp.json()["dimension"] == "accuracy"


def test_version_history_snapshots_the_dimension(client: TestClient, db_session: Any) -> None:
    """Without the snapshot, history would show the CURRENT classification against
    an OLD config — and a future restore would silently reclassify."""
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    cid = client.post(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_payload()).json()["id"]
    client.patch(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks/{cid}", json={"dimension": "validity"})
    versions = client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks/{cid}/versions").json()
    # Newest first: v2 carries the override, v1 the derived default.
    assert [v["dimension"] for v in versions] == ["validity", "completeness"]


def test_monitor_kinds_derive_their_dimension(client: TestClient, db_session: Any) -> None:
    sid = _suite_id(client, db_session)
    fresh = client.post(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_freshness_payload())
    vol = client.post(f"/api/v1/suites/{sid}/checks", json=_volume_payload())
    assert fresh.json()["dimension"] == "timeliness"
    assert vol.json()["dimension"] == "completeness"

new = '''def test_export_carries_the_dq_dimension(client: TestClient, db_session: Any) -> None:
    """ADR 0038 §6 — a document that dropped the dimension would silently

def test_import_derives_the_dimension_when_a_document_omits_it(
    client: TestClient, db_session: Any
) -> None:
    """An OLDER export has no `dimension` key at all. It must derive, exactly as if
    freshly authored — importing a whole suite as unclassified would poison the
    #889 coverage view with a gap that isn't real."""
    src = _suite_with_checks(client, db_session)
    document = client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{src}/export").json()
    for c in document["checks"]:
        c.pop("dimension", None)  # simulate a pre-ADR-0038 document
    target = _connection(db_session)

    new_id = client.post(
        "/api/v1/suites/import",
        json={"connection_id": str(target.id), "document": document},
    ).json()["id"]

    checks = client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{new_id}/checks").json()
    assert checks, "fixture must have checks for this to mean anything"
    derived = {c["expectation_type"]: c["dimension"] for c in checks}
    assert derived.get("expect_column_values_to_not_be_null") == "completeness"


def test_import_preserves_an_overridden_dimension(client: TestClient, db_session: Any) -> None:
    """A user's override must survive the round trip — otherwise export/import
    silently reverts every deliberate reclassification to the derived guess."""
    src = _suite_with_checks(client, db_session)
    document = client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{src}/export").json()
    for c in document["checks"]:
        c["dimension"] = "accuracy"  # never a derived value for these types
    target = _connection(db_session)

    new_id = client.post(
        "/api/v1/suites/import",
        json={"connection_id": str(target.id), "document": document},
    ).json()["id"]

    checks = client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{new_id}/checks").json()
    assert {c["dimension"] for c in checks} == {"accuracy"}


def test_import_rejects_a_non_canonical_dimension(client: TestClient, db_session: Any) -> None:
    src = _suite_with_checks(client, db_session)
    document = client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{src}/export").json()
    document["checks"][0]["dimension"] = "not_a_dimension"
    target = _connection(db_session)
    resp = client.post(
        "/api/v1/suites/import",
        json={"connection_id": str(target.id), "document": document},
    )
    assert resp.status_code == 422


'''

"""Catalog↔GX contract test (#205).

The check editor's ``expectationCatalog.ts`` is the frontend's source of truth
for expectation ``type`` (snake_case → GX class) and each config field name
(→ GX kwarg). The backend deliberately has NO server catalog — config is
free-form kwargs title-cased to a GX class in ``gx_runner`` — so this coupling
has zero compile-time check: a catalog typo or a GX point-release kwarg rename
ships fine and only fails at suite-run time on the worker.

This test pins the seam against the PINNED GX version, resolving each catalog
entry through the very same ``_expectation_class_name``/``getattr`` path the
runner uses. The input is ``tests/fixtures/expectation_catalog.json``, kept in
lock-step with the live TS catalog by the frontend drift-guard
(``frontend/tests/components/catalogContract.test.ts`` — regenerate with
``UPDATE_CATALOG_FIXTURE=1``). A GX bump or a catalog edit that breaks the
pairing now fails HERE, in CI, not on the worker.
"""

import functools
import json
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

import great_expectations.expectations as gxe
import pytest

from backend.app.datasources import monitors
from backend.app.datasources.gx_runner import _expectation_class_name
from backend.app.services.custom_sql import CUSTOM_SQL_EXPECTATION_TYPE, QUERY_KEY

_FIXTURE = Path(__file__).parent.parent / "fixtures" / "expectation_catalog.json"


@functools.cache
def _catalog() -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    """Parse lazily (and once): a missing/corrupt fixture must fail THESE tests,
    not abort the whole session as a collection error."""
    with _FIXTURE.open() as f:
        data: list[dict[str, Any]] = json.load(f)
    return data


def _expectations() -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    return [e for e in _catalog() if e["kind"] == "expectation"]


def _monitors() -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    return [e for e in _catalog() if e["kind"] in monitors.MONITOR_KINDS]


def _comparisons() -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    return [e for e in _catalog() if e["kind"] == "comparison"]


# Parametrization ids come from a SEPARATE minimal read that tolerates a broken
# fixture (falling back to indices), so collection itself never hard-fails.
def _expectation_params() -> list[Any]:
    try:
        entries = _expectations()
    except Exception:

has zero compile-time check: a catalog typo or a GX point-release kwarg rename
ships fine and only fails at suite-run time on the worker.

This test pins the seam against the PINNED GX version, resolving each catalog
entry through the very same ``_expectation_class_name``/``getattr`` path the
runner uses. The input is ``tests/fixtures/expectation_catalog.json``, kept in
lock-step with the live TS catalog by the frontend drift-guard
(``frontend/tests/components/catalogContract.test.ts`` — regenerate with
``UPDATE_CATALOG_FIXTURE=1``). A GX bump or a catalog edit that breaks the
pairing now fails HERE, in CI, not on the worker.

def _comparisons() -> list[dict[str, Any]]:

input.command:
cat >> backend/tests/datasources/test_catalog_gx_contract.py <<'PYEOF'


# ─────────── catalog ↔ dimension-derivation contract (ADR 0038, #124) ──────────


def _dimension_params() -> list[Any]:
    try:
        return [pytest.param(e, id=e["type"]) for e in _catalog()]
    except Exception:
        return []


@pytest.mark.parametrize("entry", _dimension_params())
def test_catalog_dimension_matches_the_backend_derivation(entry: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
    """The editor pre-fills a check's dimension from the TS catalog, but the
    BACKEND derivation is what actually gets stored (ADR 0038).

    If the two disagree, the author is shown one classification and a different
    one is persisted — a silent wrong answer with no error anywhere, and one that
    would quietly skew the #889 coverage view. There is no compile-time link
    between a TS object literal and a Python dict, so this is the only thing
    holding them together.
    """
    from backend.app.services.check_dimension import derive_dimension

    derived = derive_dimension(expectation_type=entry["type"], kind=entry["kind"])
    assert derived == entry["dimension"], (
        f"{entry['type']}: catalog says {entry['dimension']!r}, "
        f"backend derives {derived!r} — the maps have drifted"
    )


def test_every_catalog_dimension_is_canonical() -> None:
    """A catalog value outside the vocabulary would pass the editor's select and
    then 422 (or violate the table CHECK) at save time."""
    from backend.app.db.models import DQ_DIMENSIONS

    for entry in _catalog():
        assert entry["dimension"] is None or entry["dimension"] in DQ_DIMENSIONS


def test_custom_sql_is_deliberately_unclassified_in_both_maps() -> None:
    """Pinned as a decision, not left as an accident (ADR 0038 §3): an arbitrary
    SQL predicate has no derivable dimension, and guessing one would put confident
    nonsense in the scorecard."""
    from backend.app.services.check_dimension import derive_dimension

    entry = next(e for e in _catalog() if e["type"] == CUSTOM_SQL_EXPECTATION_TYPE)
    assert entry["dimension"] is None
    assert derive_dimension(expectation_type=entry["type"], kind=entry["kind"]) is None
PYEOF

 *
 * Pre-filled with the spec's derived default as a real selected value, not a
 * placeholder: what the author sees is what gets stored. Always editable, because
 * derivation is a guess about intent — the same between-check is Validity bounding
 * a percentage and Accuracy asserting a reconciled total.
 *
 * For an underivable type (custom SQL) it starts empty and stays optional, since
 * "unclassified" is a legitimate outcome the scorecard renders as a coverage gap.
 * Deliberately NOT required: forcing a guess would fill the coverage view with
 * noise, which is exactly what it exists to surface.
 */
export function DimensionField({ spec }: { spec?: ExpectationSpec }) {
  const derived = spec?.dimension;
  return (
    <Form.Item
      name="dimension"
      label="DQ dimension"
      initialValue={derived}
      extra={
        derived
          ? 'Defaulted from the check type — change it if this check means something else.'
          : 'This check type has no obvious dimension. Pick one, or leave blank to record it as unclassified.'
      }
    >
      <Select
        allowClear
        placeholder="Unclassified"
        options={DQ_DIMENSIONS.map((d: DqDimension) => ({
          value: d,
          label: `${d.charAt(0).toUpperCase()}${d.slice(1)} — ${DQ_DIMENSION_HELP[d]}`,
        }))}
      />
    </Form.Item>
  );
}

/**

input.command:
python - <<'PY'
import pathlib
p = pathlib.Path("docs/datasources-checks.md"); s = p.read_text()
anchor = "### Type names for `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type`"
new = '''### DQ dimension (ADR 0038)

Every check carries a **DQ dimension** — the quality aspect it measures. This is a
third axis, separate from the check *kind* (how it works) and the expectation type
(the specific rule):

| Dimension | Question it answers |
|---|---|
| Accuracy | Does the data match reality / a trusted source? |
| Completeness | Is all the expected data present? |
| Consistency | Do related datasets agree with each other? |
| Integrity | Do relationships between datasets hold? |
| Timeliness | Is the data recent enough? |
| Uniqueness | Are there unexpected duplicates? |
| Validity | Does the data conform to its rules and formats? |

**It is filled in for you.** The editor defaults it from the check type — a
not-null check is Completeness, a freshness monitor is Timeliness — and you can
change it at any time, including long after the check was created. Derivation is a
good guess about intent, not a fact: the same range check is *Validity* when it
bounds a percentage and *Accuracy* when it asserts a reconciled total.

Two dimensions are **never** guessed. **Accuracy** and **Integrity** can't be
inferred from the shape of a rule, and a **custom SQL** check is an arbitrary
predicate with no derivable answer at all — those start blank for you to set.

Leaving it blank is legitimate: the check is recorded as *unclassified* and shows
up as a **coverage gap** rather than being quietly filed under a dimension it
doesn't belong to. That matters because the point of dimensions is coverage —
"this table has no Timeliness checks at all" is the actionable finding, and it
would be a lie if unclassified checks were silently bucketed.

Checks created before this feature landed are unclassified until you next edit
them; they were deliberately not bulk-classified, so a derived guess is never
mistaken for someone's decision.

'''
i = s.index(anchor)
p.write_text(s[:i] + new + s[i:]); print("docs added")
PY
API=http://localhost:8000/api/v1
for s in $(curl -s $API/suites | python -c "
import sys,json
for x in json.load(sys.stdin):
    if x['name']=='dim smoke': print(x['id'])"); do curl -s -X DELETE $API/suites/$s -o /dev/null; done
curl -s $API/connections | python -c "
import sys,json,subprocess
for c in json.load(sys.stdin):
    if c['name'].startswith('dim-smoke'):
        subprocess.run(['curl','-s','-X','DELETE',f\"http://localhost:8000/api/v1/connections/{c['id']}\",'-o','/dev/null'])
print('smoke data cleaned')"

input.command:
git checkout -b feature/issue-124-dq-dimension -q 2>/dev/null || git checkout feature/issue-124-dq-dimension -q; git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
feat(checks): DQ-dimension classification (ADR 0038)

Fixes #124.

A check carried two classification axes and neither was a data-quality
*dimension*: `kind` is how the monitor works, `expectation_type` is the specific
rule. Adds the third — the semantic quality aspect a check measures — which is
what makes "we have 47 checks" legible as "your Timeliness is uncovered", and is
the axis #889's asset scorecard aggregates by.

The value is coverage, not the score. "This asset has zero Timeliness or
Uniqueness checks" is immediately actionable in a way a pass-rate never is.

Seven canonical dimensions (accuracy, completeness, consistency, integrity,
timeliness, uniqueness, validity) as a CLOSED vocabulary via a table CHECK — not
a Postgres ENUM (ALTER TYPE friction is exactly what deferring this column
avoided) and not free text (one `"timeliness "` with a trailing space and
"you have no Timeliness checks" becomes a lie).

Derived default -> stored -> overridable at any time. All three matter: nobody
should hand-classify a not-null check; storing it lets #889 GROUP BY in SQL and
lets an override survive; and derivation is a guess about intent, not a fact —
the same range check is Validity bounding a percentage and Accuracy asserting a
reconciled total.

Derivation is DELIBERATELY partial. `accuracy` and `integrity` are never derived
(whether data matches reality, or a relationship holds, is not knowable from a
rule shape) and custom SQL has no answer at all. So NULL is a real state meaning
"unclassified", which the scorecard must render as a gap. A map that guessed
would fill the scorecard with confident nonsense, which is worse than an honest
hole.

Existing rows are NOT backfilled: a derived value written by the migration would
be indistinguishable from a deliberate user classification, so a later correction
to the map could never tell "the map said so" from "a human said so".

The frontend catalog mirrors the backend map so the editor can pre-fill, and the
two are pinned together by a new contract test riding the existing #205 fixture —
without it, a divergence would show the author one classification and store
another, silently. Verified the guard fires by flipping one entry.

Migration tested up -> down -> up against the live DB; the CHECK constraint
verified to reject a bad value. Both columns are nullable with no default, so
currently-deployed code keeps working unchanged (no two-step).

input.prompt:
Review the single commit on branch `feature/issue-124-dq-dimension` in /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ (`git diff origin/main...HEAD`).

It implements GitHub issue #124 per new ADR 0038 (docs/adr/0038-dq-dimension-classification.md): a `dimension` column on checks + check_versions, a backend derivation map (`backend/app/services/check_dimension.py`), CRUD/export-import/MCP wiring, a mirrored frontend catalog field, and a check-editor select.

Be adversarial. This project's hard-won lesson is that green suites miss defects — especially silent wrong answers and state carried across operations. Probe specifically:

1. **The migration** (`backend/alembic/versions/b1c2d3e4f5a6_*`). Backward compatibility: is it genuinely safe to deploy before the code? Is the down path correct? Does the CHECK constraint name collide with anything? Is the revision id unique and the chain single-headed?
2. **Silent reclassification paths.** Any route where a check's dimension changes without the user asking: PATCH with unrelated fields, export→import round trip, the MCP tool, suite import of an old document, the check-editor edit mode, a check whose expectation_type is CHANGED by PATCH (does the dimension re-derive, stay, or go stale?). That last one is the case I'm least sure about — verify carefully what happens today and whether it's defensible.
3. **The frontend/backend mirror.** Is the contract test genuinely load-bearing for ALL entries including custom SQL and comparison? Can the catalog and map drift in a way the test misses (e.g. an entry present in one and absent in the other)?
4. **Validation.** Can a non-canonical dimension reach the DB by any path (MCP, import, PATCH, direct service call)? Is the CHECK constraint the last line or the only line?
5. **check_versions**: is the snapshot correct on create and update? Does a no-op PATCH still avoid minting a version?
6. Anything in the ADR that the code does NOT actually do.

Report concrete findings with file:line. Do not fix anything.

content.text:
## Reviewing

`git diff origin/main...HEAD` on `feature/issue-124-dq-dimension`. Note the branch is **4 commits ahead of `origin/main`**, not one — `be5b20a7` is the dimension work, but `66fccc7a`/`9cc90599`/`d14f7476` (flat-file freshness sniff + description rename) are also in this diff. This review covers the dimension commit only.

**Clean on the items I could fully verify:**
- **Migration `b1c2d3e4f5a6`** is sound. Revision id is unique, `down_revision: a3b4c5d6e7f8` exists, and the tree is single-headed (35 revisions, no branching `down_revision`, single head = this one). Both columns are nullable with no server default and no narrowing, so deploying ahead of the code is genuinely safe — old code never writes the column, and `dimension IN (...)` is not violated by NULL. The `dimension_valid` name doesn't collide (`checks` already carries `kind_valid` and `comparison_source_presence`). The bare-name create/drop pair is symmetric: alembic's `SchemaObjects.metadata()` pulls `naming_convention` off `target_metadata` (set in `backend/alembic/env.py:17`) for **both** `create_check_constraint` and `drop_constraint`, so both resolve to `ck_checks_dimension_valid` — matching the model at `backend/app/db/models.py:426` and the precedent in `9c59b6a44f33`.
- **Validation is defence-in-depth, not constraint-only.** Every write path funnels through `validate_dimension`: REST create/update (`check_service.py:610`, `:747`), MCP `create_check` (via `check_service.create_check`), and import (`suite_io_service.py:228`). MCP has no `update_check` tool, and there is no version-*restore* endpoint yet, so no unguarded path exists today. `update_check` validates before mutating, and the test asserts a rejected PATCH leaves nothing dirty.
- **`check_versions` snapshots correctly** on create and update (`check_service.py:538`), and the no-op-PATCH guard still holds — `dimension` is set inside the same `if ... is not None` block before the `session.is_modified(check)` gate.

---

## Important (80-89)

### 1. Export→import silently classifies every pre-migration check — NULL does not round-trip (confidence 88)
`backend/app/services/suite_io_service.py:82` and `:225-228`

Export always emits the key, including `"dimension": null`. Import then reads `c.get("dimension")` → `None` → **derives**. There is no way for the importer to distinguish "the document is older and omits the field" from "the document explicitly says this check is unclassified."

ADR 0038 §5 is explicit that existing rows are deliberately **not** backfilled, because "a derived value written by this migration would be indistinguishable from a deliberate user classification." Import re-creates exactly that problem by another door:

> A check created before this migration has `dimension = NULL`. Export the suite → `{"expectation_type": "expect_column_values_to_not_be_null", "dimension": null}` → import → the new check is stored as `completeness`, attributable to nobody, indistinguishable from a human override, and permanently immune to a later correction of the derivation map.

That is every check currently in prod. The ADR's own §6 wording is *"Absent on import (an older export) → derived"* — absent, not null-valued.

The test suite misses it by construction: `test_import_derives_the_dimension_when_a_document_omits_it` **pops** the key (`backend/tests/api/test_suites.py`, `c.pop("dimension", None)`), and `test_import_preserves_an_overridden_dimension` sets a non-null value. No test carries `dimension: null` through for a *derivable* type — the only null that survives today is custom SQL, where derivation happens to also return `None`, so the round trip looks correct for the wrong reason.

### 2. Changing a check's expectation type in the editor stores the OLD type's dimension, under help text that says otherwise (confidence 85)
`frontend/src/pages/CheckEdit.tsx:135`, `:212`; `frontend/src/components/checks/checkFormFields.tsx:117-137`

This is the case you flagged as least sure, and it does misbehave — on the frontend, not the backend.

`CheckEdit` seeds `dimension: check.dimension` once (`:135`) and has **no** `resetFields`/`setFieldsValue` on expectation-type change — `spec` is recomputed from `Form.useWatch('expectation_type')` (`:117`) but the `dimension` form value is untouched. The expectation-type `Select` is editable for non-monitor checks.

> Open a check of type `expect_column_values_to_not_be_null` (stored `completeness`). Change the type to `expect_column_values_to_be_unique`. `DimensionField` re-renders with `derived = 'uniqueness'` and prints *"Defaulted from the check type — change it if this check means something else"* — while the select still displays **Completeness**. `buildCheckPayload` (`checkForm.ts:77`) sends `dimension: "completeness"` explicitly. The check is now a uniqueness check classified as completeness, and it looks like a deliberate override forever.

The backend half (`check_service.update_check` does not re-derive when `expectation_type` changes) is defensible in isolation — it cannot tell a derived value from a user's override, so leaving it alone is the conservative choice, and PATCHing type + dimension together is expressible. But nothing anywhere re-derives, and the UI actively transmits the stale value while claiming it's the type's default. The two halves together produce a silent wrong answer. `CheckNew` is not affected: type selection is a prior step and `form.resetFields()` fires on `expectationType` change (`CheckNew.tsx:63-65`).

No test covers a type change in edit mode; the two new `CheckEdit.test.tsx` cases only cover prefill of a stored value and of a stored NULL.

### 3. The select's `allowClear` is a no-op on edit and does the *opposite* on create (confidence 84)
`frontend/src/components/checks/checkFormFields.tsx:141`; `frontend/src/components/checks/checkForm.ts:77`; `frontend/src/pages/CheckEdit.tsx:157`

The select renders `allowClear` with placeholder `"Unclassified"`, and for underivable types the help text reads *"leave blank to record it as unclassified"* (`:137`). But `resolve_dimension`'s documented asymmetry means `None` is "derive", never "clear":

- **Edit, non-comparison:** clearing → `values.dimension === undefined` → `buildCheckPayload` emits `dimension: undefined` → key omitted from JSON → backend leaves the field **unchanged**. The user sees an empty select and a `"saved"` toast; the old value is still there on reload.
- **Edit, comparison:** `CheckEdit.tsx:157` sends `dimension: null` explicitly, which the backend also reads as "not provided" → unchanged. Same silent no-op.
- **Create:** clearing a pre-filled default → omitted → backend **derives it back**. The user explicitly asked for unclassified and got `completeness`.

The service docstring calls the un-clearability intentional, which is a reasonable v1 position — but the UI then must not offer a clear affordance, and the help text must not promise "leave blank to record it as unclassified" on a path where blank means "derive."

### 4. The catalog↔map contract test only pins one direction (confidence 82)
`backend/tests/datasources/test_catalog_gx_contract.py:159-176`

`_dimension_params()` parametrizes over `_catalog()` — the frontend-generated fixture. Every fixture entry is asserted against `derive_dimension`, so a **catalog** entry that drifts (or one added with no backend mapping) fails. The reverse does not: a key added to `_BY_EXPECTATION_TYPE` or `_BY_KIND` in `check_dimension.py` that has no catalog entry is never visited by any assertion.

That is the direction that produces the silent wrong answer you're guarding against — the backend map is the authority at write time, so a backend-only entry means the editor shows the author nothing (or an empty select) while the backend stores a classification they never saw. The fix is a set-comparison test (`set(_BY_EXPECTATION_TYPE) ⊆ {e["type"] for e in _catalog()}`, and likewise for `_BY_KIND` keys vs catalog kinds), not another per-entry parametrize.

Today the maps happen to agree — all 8 `_BY_EXPECTATION_TYPE` keys and all 4 `_BY_KIND` keys are represented in the catalog — so this is a latent gap, not a live defect. Worth noting that custom SQL and both comparison shapes *are* genuinely covered: custom SQL is pinned by its own dedicated test (`:171`), and `comparison:records`/`comparison:columns` ride the parametrized test via the `_BY_KIND['comparison']` fallback.

---

## Minor (below the reporting bar, noted for completeness)

- ADR §1 says the column is stored as `TEXT`; the model and migration use `String(32)` (VARCHAR(32)). Harmless for the current seven values, but an eighth dimension now needs a type change as well as a constraint change.
- `backend/app/mcp/server.py:95-113` — `_reject_nul` splats `**config` after `"dimension": dimension`, so a config key literally named `dimension` shadows it. Pre-existing pattern (same is true of `name`/`kind`), and unreachable in practice since `validate_dimension` rejects anything non-canonical.

content.content:
def import_suite(
    payload: SuiteImportRequest,
    current_user: Annotated[User, Depends(get_current_user)],
    db: Annotated[Session, Depends(get_db)],
) -> SuiteRead:
    # Like create_suite: any authenticated user may import; the new suite is
    # owned by them. Thresholds/config round-trip exactly (Decimal in/out).
    doc = payload.document
    suite = suite_io.import_suite(
        db,
        version=doc.version,
        name=doc.name,
        description=doc.description,
        checks=[c.model_dump() for c in doc.checks],
        connection_id=payload.connection_id,
        created_by=current_user.id,
    )
    return SuiteRead.model_validate(suite)


# ───────────────────────── column profiler (no persistence) ─────────

content.text: Confirmed — `model_dump()` always emits the key, so the service can't distinguish "absent" from "explicitly null". Fixing all four findings:

input.command:
python - <<'PY'
import pathlib
# --- #1a: only forward `dimension` when the payload actually set it
p = pathlib.Path("backend/app/api/v1/suites.py"); s = p.read_text()
s = s.replace("""        checks=[c.model_dump() for c in doc.checks],""",
"""        # `dimension` is dropped when the payload did not SET it, so the service
        # can tell "an older document omits the field" (→ derive) from "this
        # document says the check is unclassified" (→ keep NULL). model_dump()
        # alone emits the key either way, which would classify every pre-ADR-0038
        # check on import — exactly the backfill ADR 0038 §5 forbids.
        checks=[
            {
                k: v
                for k, v in c.model_dump().items()
                if k != "dimension" or "dimension" in c.model_fields_set
            }
            for c in doc.checks
        ],""", 1)
p.write_text(s)

# --- #1b: service honours an explicit null
p = pathlib.Path("backend/app/services/suite_io_service.py"); s = p.read_text()
s = s.replace("""            # An older document has no `dimension` key at all; derive it so an
            # import behaves like a fresh authoring rather than landing a whole
            # suite as unclassified.
            dimension=resolve_dimension(
                expectation_type=c["expectation_type"],
                kind=c["kind"],
                explicit=validate_dimension(c.get("dimension")),
            ),""",
"""            # Key ABSENT (an older document) → derive, so an import behaves like
            # fresh authoring. Key PRESENT — including an explicit null → take it
            # verbatim: a null means "this check is unclassified", and deriving
            # over it would re-create the very backfill ADR 0038 §5 forbids for
            # every check that predates the migration.
            dimension=(
                validate_dimension(c["dimension"])
                if "dimension" in c
                else derive_dimension(expectation_type=c["expectation_type"], kind=c["kind"])
            ),""", 1)
s = s.replace("from backend.app.services.check_dimension import resolve_dimension",
              "from backend.app.services.check_dimension import derive_dimension", 1)
p.write_text(s); print("finding 1 fixed")
PY
grep -n "resolve_dimension\|derive_dimension" backend/app/services/suite_io_service.py

def test_no_backend_mapping_is_missing_from_the_catalog() -> None:
    """The reverse direction of the drift guard, and the dangerous one.

    The per-entry test above walks the CATALOG, so a catalog entry that drifts is
    caught. A key added to the BACKEND map with no catalog entry is not — and that
    is the direction that produces a silent wrong answer: the backend is the
    authority at write time, so the editor would show the author an empty select
    while a classification they never saw gets stored.

    catalog_kinds = {e["kind"] for e in _catalog()}

    orphan_types = set(check_dimension._BY_EXPECTATION_TYPE) - catalog_types
    orphan_kinds = set(check_dimension._BY_KIND) - catalog_kinds
    assert not orphan_types, f"backend maps types absent from the catalog: {sorted(orphan_types)}"
    assert not orphan_kinds, f"backend maps kinds absent from the catalog: {sorted(orphan_kinds)}"
PYEOF

content.text: Now tests for the round-trip fix — the case that was silently wrong:

input.command:
python - <<'PY'
import pathlib
p = pathlib.Path("backend/tests/api/test_suites.py"); s = p.read_text()
anchor = "def test_import_rejects_a_non_canonical_dimension("
new = '''def test_import_preserves_an_explicitly_unclassified_check(
    client: TestClient, db_session: Any
) -> None:
    """The null must round-trip, not derive.

    Export always emits the key, so a check that predates ADR 0038 exports as
    `"dimension": null`. If import treated that the same as an absent key, every
    pre-migration check would be silently classified on the way through —
    attributable to nobody and indistinguishable from a human override, which is
    exactly the backfill §5 forbids. `null` present ≠ key absent.
    """
    src = _suite_with_checks(client, db_session)
    document = client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{src}/export").json()
    for c in document["checks"]:
        c["dimension"] = None  # a derivable type, explicitly unclassified
    target = _connection(db_session)

    new_id = client.post(
        "/api/v1/suites/import",
        json={"connection_id": str(target.id), "document": document},
    ).json()["id"]

    checks = client.get(f"/api/v1/suites/{new_id}/checks").json()
    assert checks
    assert {c["dimension"] for c in checks} == {None}


'''
i = s.index(anchor)
p.write_text(s[:i] + new + s[i:]); print("ok")
PY

python - <<'PY'
import pathlib
p = pathlib.Path("docs/adr/0038-dq-dimension-classification.md"); s = p.read_text()
s = s.replace("Stored as `TEXT` with a table `CHECK` over the vocabulary",
              "Stored as `VARCHAR(32)` with a table `CHECK` over the vocabulary", 1)
s = s.replace("""- **Export/import** — `dimension` rides the check document. Absent on import (an older export) → derived, exactly as if freshly authored. The export version does **not** bump: adding an optional field is backward-compatible in both directions, and `EXPORT_VERSION` is documented to bump only on an *incompatible* shape change.""",
"""- **Export/import** — `dimension` rides the check document, and the two cases are distinguished: **absent** (an older export) → derived, exactly as if freshly authored; **present, including an explicit `null`** → taken verbatim, so an unclassified check stays unclassified. Conflating them would re-create §5's forbidden backfill by another door — export always emits the key, so every pre-migration check would be silently classified on the way through. The export version does **not** bump: adding an optional field is backward-compatible in both directions, and `EXPORT_VERSION` is documented to bump only on an *incompatible* shape change.
- **Changing a check's expectation type** re-derives the dimension in the editor (the create page already resets the whole form on a type change). Keeping the old value would leave the select showing a classification the help text simultaneously claims is "defaulted from the check type" — and it would then be sent explicitly, so a uniqueness check filed as completeness would look like a deliberate override forever. The backend does not re-derive on PATCH: it cannot tell a derived value from an override, so leaving it is the conservative choice, and the editor sends the corrected value.""", 1)
p.write_text(s); print("ADR corrected")
PY
git add -A && git commit -q -m "fix(checks): null must round-trip, and a type change must re-derive

Four review findings on the dimension work, all fixed in-PR.

1. EXPORT->IMPORT SILENTLY CLASSIFIED EVERY PRE-MIGRATION CHECK. Export always
   emits the key, so a check that predates ADR 0038 exports as
   \`\"dimension\": null\` -- and import read null as \"not specified, derive it\".
   That re-created by another door exactly the backfill \xc2\xa75 forbids: every check
   currently in prod would come back classified, attributable to nobody and
   indistinguishable from a human override. Absent key and explicit null are now
   distinguished (the route drops \`dimension\` unless \`model_fields_set\` says the
   payload set it). The existing tests missed it by construction -- one POPS the
   key, the other sets a non-null value, and the only null that round-tripped
   was custom SQL, where derivation also returns None, so it looked right for
   the wrong reason.

2. Changing a check's expectation type in the editor kept the OLD type's
   dimension while the help text below claimed the value was \"defaulted from the
   check type\" -- and buildCheckPayload then sent the stale value explicitly, so
   a uniqueness check ended up filed as completeness and looked like a
   deliberate override forever. The editor now re-derives on a type change,
   mirroring the create page's reset.

3. \`allowClear\` was a no-op on edit and did the OPPOSITE on create: blank means
   \"derive\" to the backend, never \"clear\", so clearing a pre-filled default
   restored it. The affordance now appears only where blank genuinely stores
   NULL -- a type with no derived default.

4. The catalog<->map drift guard only walked the CATALOG, so a backend-only
   mapping was never visited. That is the dangerous direction: the backend is
   the authority at write time, so the editor would show an empty select while
   storing a classification the author never saw. Added the reverse
   set-comparison; verified it fires.

Also corrects the ADR: it said TEXT, the column is VARCHAR(32)." && echo committed

attachment.snippet:
1	# ADR 0038 — DQ-dimension classification on checks: seven canonical dimensions, derived default, stored and overridable
2	
3	- **Status:** Accepted
4	- **Date:** 2026-07-19
5	- **Deciders:** @TheurgicDuke771
6	- **Related:** [0005](0005-severity-tiers-and-health-score.md) (severity tiers + the health-score formula the scorecard reuses), [0012](0012-monitor-kind-seam.md) (`check.kind` — the axis this one is orthogonal to), [0015](0015-two-connection-comparison-check-model.md) (`comparison` kind), [0019](0019-custom-sql-checks.md) (custom SQL — the one authoring path with no derivable dimension), [0036](0036-connection-anchored-check-engines.md) (§4 names this work; see *Relationship to ADR 0036* below), [0037](0037-workspace-visible-asset-identity.md) (the workspace-true aggregate rule the scorecard must obey). Issues: [#124](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/124) (this ADR), [#889](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/889) (the asset scorecard that consumes it).
7	
8	## Context
9	
10	A check today carries two classification axes, and neither is a data-quality *dimension*:
11	
12	| Field | Axis | Example |
13	|---|---|---|
14	| `kind` (ADR 0012) | *how* the monitor works | `expectation` / `freshness` / `comparison` |
15	| `expectation_type` | the specific GX rule | `expect_column_values_to_be_unique` |
16	
17	A **DQ dimension** is a third, orthogonal axis: the *semantic category of quality* a check measures. It is what makes "we have 47 checks" legible as "your Timeliness is uncovered" — and it is the input the asset scorecard (#889) aggregates by.
18	
19	The value is not primarily the score. It is **coverage**: "this asset has zero Timeliness and zero Uniqueness checks" is immediately actionable in a way a pass-rate never is, and CLAUDE.md §5 already records that most real incidents are freshness/volume — i.e. exactly the dimensions teams are least likely to have covered.
20	
21	Unlike the ADR-0005 severity columns or ADR-0012's `kind`/`metric_value`, a `dimension` column is **pure widening** — nullable, additive, no data rewrite, no two-step deploy — so there was never schema-timing pressure to rush it. That is why #124 was deliberately held back from the Week-3 migration and gets a deliberate design pass now.
22	
23	## Decision
24	
25	### 1. Seven canonical dimensions, closed vocabulary
26	
27	`accuracy`, `completeness`, `consistency`, `integrity`, `timeliness`, `uniqueness`, `validity`.
28	
29	The canonical six (completeness, uniqueness, validity, accuracy, consistency, timeliness) plus **integrity** — referential/relational correctness, which the six fold into "consistency" and which DataQ can express distinctly once cross-dataset comparison exists (ADR 0015).
30	
31	Stored as `VARCHAR(32)` with a table `CHECK` over the vocabulary, matching the `CHECK_KINDS` idiom (`models._in_check`) — **not** a Postgres `ENUM`. An enum type needs `ALTER TYPE` to extend, which is exactly the migration friction this column was deferred to avoid; a `CHECK` constraint is a one-line ALTER and stays visible in the model file next to the other vocabularies.
32	
33	**Closed, not free-form.** A free-text dimension makes the scorecard's coverage view meaningless the first time someone types "Timeliness " with a trailing space — you cannot report "you have no Timeliness checks" against a set you don't control. The cost is that adding an eighth dimension is a migration; that is the right trade for a reporting axis.
34	
35	### 2. Derived default, stored value, user-overridable at any time
36	
37	Three properties, and all three are load-bearing:
38	
39	- **Derived** — on create, if the check's type maps to a dimension, that is the default. Nobody should have to hand-classify `expect_column_values_to_not_be_null` as Completeness.
40	- **Stored** — the resolved value is written to `checks.dimension`, not recomputed on read. This is what lets #889 aggregate with a SQL `GROUP BY` instead of joining through a Python map, and what makes an override survive.
41	- **Overridable at any time** — at create *and* by later PATCH. Derivation is a good guess about intent, not a fact: the same `expect_column_values_to_be_between` is Validity when it bounds a percentage and Accuracy when it asserts a reconciled total.
42	
43	Rejected: **pure derivation** (no column) — no override, and #889 loses SQL aggregation. Rejected: **pure user-set** — every existing check and most new ones land unclassified, so the coverage view is empty until someone backfills by hand, which nobody does.
44	
45	### 3. Derivation is deliberately partial, and `NULL` is a real state
46	
47	| Check type | Dimension | Why |
48	|---|---|---|
49	| `expect_column_values_to_not_be_null` | `completeness` | |
50	| `expect_table_row_count_to_be_between` | `completeness` | "is all the data here" |
51	| `monitor:volume` | `completeness` | a short load is missing data |
52	| `expect_column_values_to_be_unique` | `uniqueness` | |
53	| `expect_column_values_to_be_between` | `validity` | conforms to a rule |
54	| `expect_column_values_to_be_in_set` | `validity` | |
55	| `expect_column_values_to_match_regex` | `validity` | |
56	| `expect_column_value_lengths_to_be_between` | `validity` | |
57	| `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` | `validity` | |
58	| `monitor:freshness` | `timeliness` | |
59	| `monitor:schema_drift` | `consistency` | structural stability over time |
60	| `comparison:records` / `comparison:columns` | `consistency` | cross-dataset agreement (ADR 0015) |
61	| custom SQL (`unexpected_rows_expectation`) | **none** | arbitrary predicate — unknowable |
62	
63	**`accuracy` and `integrity` are never derived.** No generic GX expectation can tell you whether data matches reality, or whether a relationship holds. They exist for the author to select — most often on a custom-SQL check, which is precisely the path with no derivable answer. Pretending to derive them would populate the scorecard with confident nonsense, which is worse than an honest gap.
64	
65	So `dimension` is **nullable**, and NULL means "unclassified" — a state the scorecard must render as a gap, never silently bucket. Existing checks keep NULL (see §5).
66	
67	### 4. Orthogonal to `kind` and to `engine`
68	
69	`kind` is *how the monitor works*, `dimension` is *what quality aspect it measures*, `engine` (ADR 0036) is *what evaluates it*. All three vary independently: a `freshness` kind is Timeliness whether GX or DMF runs it; a `comparison` kind is Consistency by default but Accuracy when the baseline is a source of truth.
70	
71	The derivation map is therefore keyed on `expectation_type` **with a `kind` fallback**, not on `kind` alone — `expectation` covers a dozen different dimensions, so keying on kind would collapse them.
72	
73	### 5. Backward compatibility
74	
75	Additive nullable column on `checks` **and** on `check_versions` (history must round-trip the field, or restoring an old version silently reclassifies a check).
76	
77	**Existing rows are left NULL, not backfilled.** A backfill would be indistinguishable from a deliberate user classification, so a later correction to the derivation map could never tell "the map said so" from "a human said so". Instead, existing checks surface as unclassified and are classified on next edit — visible, attributable, and consistent with NULL being a real state. Users who want them classified in bulk can re-save; a bulk-classify action is out of scope.
78	
79	### 6. Surfaces
80	
81	- **Check editor** — a dimension select, pre-filled with the derived default, always editable. The derived default is shown as the selected value rather than as a placeholder, so what you see is what gets stored.

... [25 lines truncated] ...

input.command:
git push -u origin feature/issue-124-dq-dimension -q 2>&1 | tail -2; gh pr create --base main --title "feat(checks): DQ-dimension classification (ADR 0038)" --milestone "v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fixes #124. Adds [ADR 0038](docs/adr/0038-dq-dimension-classification.md).

> **Note:** this branch also carries the #944 flat-file follow-ups (the mostly-unparseable-column guard and the sniff-read bound). Merge #944 first and this diff shrinks to the dimension work.

## Why

A check carried two classification axes and neither was a data-quality *dimension*: `kind` is how the monitor works, `expectation_type` is the specific rule. This adds the third — the semantic quality aspect a check measures.

**The value is coverage, not the score.** "This asset has zero Timeliness or Uniqueness checks" is immediately actionable in a way a pass-rate never is — and CLAUDE.md §5 already records that most real incidents are freshness/volume, i.e. exactly the dimensions teams are least likely to have covered. It's also the axis #889's scorecard aggregates by.

## Design

**Seven canonical dimensions, closed vocabulary** via a table `CHECK` — not a Postgres `ENUM` (`ALTER TYPE` friction is exactly what deferring this column avoided) and not free text (one `"timeliness "` with a trailing space and "you have no Timeliness checks" becomes a lie).

**Derived default → stored → overridable at any time.** All three matter: nobody should hand-classify a not-null check; storing it lets #889 `GROUP BY` in SQL and lets an override survive; and derivation is a guess about intent, not a fact — the same range check is Validity bounding a percentage and Accuracy asserting a reconciled total.

**Derivation is deliberately partial.** `accuracy` and `integrity` are never derived (whether data matches reality, or a relationship holds, isn't knowable from a rule shape) and custom SQL has no answer at all. So `NULL` is a real state meaning *unclassified*, which the scorecard renders as a gap. A map that guessed would fill the scorecard with confident nonsense — worse than an honest hole.

**Existing rows are not backfilled.** A derived value written by the migration would be indistinguishable from a deliberate user classification, so a later correction to the map could never tell "the map said so" from "a human said so".

## What review caught — four findings, all fixed in-PR

1. **Export→import silently classified every pre-migration check.** Export always emits the key, so a pre-ADR-0038 check exports as `"dimension": null` — and import read null as "derive". That re-created §5's forbidden backfill by another door, for **every check currently in prod**. Absent key and explicit null are now distinguished. The existing tests missed it *by construction*: one **pops** the key, the other sets a non-null value, and the only null that round-tripped was custom SQL — where derivation also returns `None`, so it looked right for the wrong reason.
2. **Changing a check's expectation type kept the old type's dimension** while the help text below claimed the value was "defaulted from the check type" — then sent it explicitly, so a uniqueness check filed as completeness looked like a deliberate override forever.
3. **`allowClear` was a no-op on edit and did the opposite on create** (blank means "derive", never "clear", so clearing a default restored it). The affordance now appears only where blank genuinely stores NULL.
4. **The drift guard only walked one direction.** A backend-only mapping was never visited — the dangerous direction, since the backend is the authority at write time, so the editor would show an empty select while storing a classification the author never saw.

## Verification

- Migration tested **up → down → up** against the live DB; the CHECK constraint verified to actually reject a bad value. Both columns nullable with no default, so deploying ahead of the code is safe (no two-step).
- **Drift guards mutation-verified**, both directions — flipped a map entry and added a ghost entry, confirmed each fires with a readable message.
- The edit-mode re-derivation test was mutation-verified too.
- Live API smoke: derived (`completeness`/`uniqueness`/`timeliness`), override (`accuracy`), bad value → 422, export/import round-trip.
- Live UI: the select renders pre-filled as "Uniqueness — Are there unexpected duplicates?" with its help text.
- Backend **2549 passed, 97.16%**; frontend **612 passed**. Black/Ruff/mypy/Bandit/ESLint/Prettier/tsc clean.

One test I wrote specifically to guard against silent reclassification **found a real bug in my own implementation** — `buildCheckPayload` fell back to the spec default, so renaming a deliberately-unclassified check would have reclassified it.

## Scope note

Per ADR 0038's *Relationship to ADR 0036*: this lands the dimension axis and the backend derivation map — the seed of 0036 §4's engine-aware backend catalog — **without** moving the frontend catalog server-side, since no native engine exists yet to define that interface against.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

content.text:
#124 is built and up as [#945](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/945).

## Where group 2 stands

**Done: #124 — DQ-dimension classification.** ADR 0038 + migration + derivation map + CRUD/export-import/MCP + editor select, verified against the live app and DB.

**Next: #889 — the scorecard**, in two PRs as planned: the rollup unification first (its own AC), then the per-dimension coverage + weighted score.

## Three things worth your attention

**1. The most valuable finding was an ADR violation I'd shipped into my own PR.** Export always emits the `dimension` key, so a pre-migration check exports as `"dimension": null` — and import read null as "derive". That silently classified **every check currently in prod** on any export/import round trip: attributable to nobody, indistinguishable from a human override, and permanently immune to a later correction of the map. It re-created by another door the exact backfill ADR 0038 §5 forbids.

What makes it worth flagging: **my tests missed it by construction.** One test *pops* the key, the other sets a non-null value — and the only null that round-tripped was custom SQL, where derivation also returns `None`, so it looked correct for the wrong reason.

**2. A test I wrote to guard against silent reclassification caught a real bug in my own code** — `buildCheckPayload` fell back to the spec's default, so renaming a deliberately-unclassified check would have reclassified it to `validity`. That's the third time this session the guard found the thing it was written for.

**3. The catalog↔backend mirror is a genuine drift risk, now pinned both ways.** There's no compile-time link between a TS object literal and a Python dict, and a divergence would show the author one classification while storing another. I mutation-verified both directions fire.

## Two caveats to carry into #889

- The scorecard must treat `NULL` as a **coverage gap**, never bucket it — and per ADR 0037 the aggregate has to be **workspace-true** (all composing suites, one number for every viewer). `asset_view_service`'s docstring literally cites "#889's two-verdicts-on-one-page problem" as the reason.
- Existing checks are unclassified until next edited. On a real workspace the first scorecard will look sparse. That's the honest design, but you may want a bulk-classify action later — say so and I'll file it rather than let it surface as a surprise.

Also still open: [#944](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/944) (flat-file follow-ups). #945 is stacked on it, so #944 should merge first — I'll watch CI on both and merge in order before starting the rollup unification.

new = ("| ✅ | [#124](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/124) DQ-dimension classification on checks — **CLOSED 2026-07-19** "
 "([#945](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/945), **ADR [0038](../docs/adr/0038-dq-dimension-classification.md)**). Seven canonical "
 "dimensions as a CLOSED vocabulary (table `CHECK`, not a Postgres ENUM — `ALTER TYPE` friction is what deferring this column avoided; not free text "
 "— one `\"timeliness \"` and \"you have no Timeliness checks\" becomes a lie). Derived default → **stored** (so #889 can `GROUP BY` in SQL and an "
 "override survives) → overridable at any time. Derivation is **deliberately partial**: `accuracy`/`integrity` are never derived and custom SQL has no "
 "answer, so **NULL means unclassified** and renders as a coverage gap. Existing rows **not backfilled** — a derived value would be indistinguishable "
 "from a human decision. **The review's best catch was an ADR violation in the PR itself**: export always emits the key, so a pre-migration check "
 "exports as `\"dimension\": null` and import read null as \"derive\" — silently classifying **every check in prod** on any round trip, re-creating the "
 "§5 backfill by another door. My tests missed it *by construction* (one POPS the key; the only null that round-tripped was custom SQL, where "
 "derivation also returns None — right for the wrong reason). Also fixed: stale dimension on an expectation-type change, a misleading `allowClear`, and "
 "a one-directional drift guard (a backend-only mapping was never visited — the dangerous direction, since the backend is the authority at write time) | Theme 2 |")
s = s[:i] + new + s[j:]
p.write_text(s); print("ok")

input.command:
python - <<'PY'
import pathlib
p = pathlib.Path("docs/progress.md"); s = p.read_text()
s = s.replace("| **Open issues** | **47** in the **`v1.1 Backlog`** milestone (82 open repo-wide)",
              "| **Open issues** | **49** in the **`v1.1 Backlog`** milestone (83 open repo-wide)", 1)
p.write_text(s); print("snapshot updated")
PY
python - <<'PY'
import pathlib
p = pathlib.Path("CLAUDE.md"); s = p.read_text()
old = "**Next milestone:** **v1.1 Week 4 — `schema_drift` monitor kind (due 2026-08-01) — IN PROGRESS, 4/8.**"
new = "**Next milestone:** **v1.1 Week 4 — `schema_drift` monitor kind (due 2026-08-01) — IN PROGRESS, 7/8;** only **#889** (asset DQ scorecard) remains. **#124 shipped 2026-07-19 (ADR 0038, #945)** — a third classification axis on a check (the quality aspect it measures), orthogonal to `kind` (how the monitor works) and `engine` (what evaluates it). Seven canonical dimensions as a **closed** vocabulary via table `CHECK`; derived default → **stored** (so #889 aggregates in SQL and an override survives) → overridable any time. Derivation is **deliberately partial** — `accuracy`/`integrity` are never derivable from a rule shape and custom SQL is an arbitrary predicate — so **NULL means unclassified and renders as a coverage gap**, never silently bucketed; existing rows are **not backfilled**, because a derived value is indistinguishable from a human decision. **The review's best catch was an ADR violation inside the PR that added the ADR:** export always emits the key, so a pre-migration check exports as `\"dimension\": null`, and import read null as \"derive\" — silently classifying **every check in prod** on any export/import round trip, re-creating §5's forbidden backfill by another door. **The tests missed it by construction** — one POPS the key, the other sets a non-null value, and the only null that round-tripped was custom SQL, where derivation *also* returns `None`, so it looked correct for the wrong reason. **Lesson: a test whose fixture can't express the failing case proves nothing, and \"passes\" is not the same as \"passes for the right reason\".** Also caught: a stale dimension when the expectation type changed (help text claimed \"defaulted from the check type\" while sending the old one), a misleading `allowClear` (blank means *derive*, never *clear*), and a drift guard that only walked one direction — a backend-only mapping was never visited, which is the dangerous direction since the backend is the authority at write time. "
assert old in s
s = s.replace(old, new, 1)
p.write_text(s); print("CLAUDE.md updated")
PY
git add -A && git commit -q -m "docs: close #124 in the W4 tracker (DQ dimensions shipped)

W4 now 7/8 — only #889 (the asset scorecard) remains. Ticks the epic checkbox
and re-counts the backlog to 49 (#942/#943 filed from the flat-file audit).

Records the lesson worth keeping: the review's best catch was an ADR violation
inside the PR that ADDED the ADR, and my tests missed it BY CONSTRUCTION — one
popped the key rather than setting it null, and the only null that round-tripped
was the case where derivation also returns None, so it looked right for the wrong
reason." && git push -u origin docs/w4-124-complete -q && gh pr create --base main --title "docs: close #124 in the W4 tracker (DQ dimensions shipped)" --milestone "v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Docs-only, per the PR-template step after #945 merged. **W4 is now 7/8** — only #889 (the asset scorecard) remains.

- #124 row → ✅; section count 6/8 → 7/8; epic #597 checkbox ticked.
- Snapshot re-counted: 49 backlog (#942/#943 filed from the flat-file audit), 83 open repo-wide.
- CLAUDE.md §13 milestone headline refreshed.

Records the lesson worth not re-learning: the review's best catch was **an ADR violation inside the PR that added the ADR** — export always emits the `dimension` key, so a pre-migration check exports as `"dimension": null`, and import read null as "derive", silently classifying every check in prod on any round trip.

And *why* it survived: the tests missed it **by construction**. One popped the key rather than setting it null; the only null that round-tripped was custom SQL, where derivation *also* returns `None` — so it passed for the wrong reason. A test whose fixture can't express the failing case proves nothing.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"
